parts.content: Explain how consumer income influences the demand of a product.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 


**Definition (Key Terms)**  
- **Demand:** The quantity of a good or service that consumers are willing and able to purchase at various price levels in a given time period.  
- **Consumer Income (Disposable Income):** The total amount of income available to consumers after direct taxes have been paid.  
- **Income Elasticity of Demand (YED)** Measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in consumer income.  

**Explanation/Economic Theory**  
- **Income Elasticity of Demand (YED)**  
  - A positive YED (greater than zero) indicates a normal good, whereas a negative YED indicates an inferior good.  
  - Magnitude of YED highlights how significantly demand changes with income fluctuations (luxury goods tend to have YED > 1).

- **Normal Good vs. Inferior Good:** A normal good experiences increased demand when consumer income rises, whereas an inferior good experiences decreased demand when consumer income rises.

- **Income Effect on Normal Goods**  
  - For normal goods, higher income allows consumers to purchase more.
  - Since consumers are now able to purchase more quantities of the good at all possible price levels, the demand curve shifts outwards ($D_1 \rightarrow D_2$).  
  - This shift in demand reflects consumers’ greater ability to spend on higher-quality or more expensive items.

- **Income Effect on Inferior Goods**  
  - Inferior goods see a decrease in demand when incomes rise, as consumers switch to higher-quality alternatives.  
  - Since consumers are now willing to purchase less quantities of the good at all possible price levels, the demand curve shifts outwards ($D_1 \rightarrow D_3$).  

- **Opposite effect**
	- The opposite effect occurs as well. When income decreases, consumers switch from normal goods to inferior goods.
	- In such case, the demand, at all possible price levels, will increase for inferior goods, shifting their demand curve to the right ($D_1 \rightarrow D_2$).
	- Contrarily, the demand, at all possible price levels, will decrease for normal goods, shifting their demand curve to the left ($D_1 \rightarrow D_3$).
	- All these shifts are can be seen in the figure below.

**Diagram (Shifts in Demand for a Normal vs Inferior Good)**  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e79352d1-f0e7-4e14-93a3-d680e4f44342)

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that governments should consider PED when placing indirect taxes on demerit goods.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions 
- **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED):** Measures the responsiveness of the quantity demanded of a good to a change in its price. Calculated as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.  
- **Indirect Tax:** A tax levied on the expenditure of goods or services, typically imposed on producers (who pass some or all of the tax burden onto consumers through higher prices).  
- **Demerit Goods:** Goods that are not socially desirable but are over provided by the market.

### Economic Theory

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f47ddde7-6636-4055-8044-e247dc61c663)

- As seen in the diagrams above, when a government imposes an **indirect tax**, the supply curve effectively shifts upwards by the amount of the tax per unit ($S \rightarrow S_{+tax}$).  
- The new higher price reduces the **quantity demanded** ($Q_m \rightarrow Q_tax$) depending on the PED of the product:  
 - If demand is **price inelastic** (PED < 1), consumers are relatively unresponsive to price changes. 
 - The tax burden falls mostly on consumers, as producers can pass on a larger portion of the tax without losing significant sales volume.
 - This is reflected by the fact that the increase in consumer price consumer pay ($P_c$) from the original market price ($P_m$) is greater than the decrease in producer post-tax price ($P_p$).
 - Government revenue is usually higher because quantity demanded does not fall drastically.  
 - If demand is **price elastic** (PED > 1), consumers are relatively responsive to price changes. 
 - A significant portion of the tax burden falls on producers, since passing on a large price increase would result in a proportionally larger drop in quantity demanded.
 - This is reflected by the fact that the increase in consumer price consumer pay ($P_c$) from the original market price ($P_m$) is lower than the decrease in producer post-tax price ($P_p$).
 - Government tax revenue may be lower because the fall in quantity demanded is large.  
- **Welfare loss**:
	- As seen by the diagram (the loss in total social surplus) arises if the tax significantly reduces the quantity traded in the market. 
	- If the product has relatively inelastic demand, the overall loss to society is typically smaller in quantity terms, but consumers bear more cost. 
	- If the product has elastic demand, quantity falls more, potentially increasing the deadweight loss but decreasing the price burden on consumers.  

- **Imposition of taxes on demerit goods** 
	- Demerit goods have negative consumption externalities: spillover costs passed on to third parties, from the consumption of a good, which is not reflected in the market price.
	- Hence, the marginal private benefit (MPB) consumers receive for the good is lower than the real marginal social benefit (MSB) society receives.
	- This is reflected in the diagram below. The demand curve represents the marginal private benefits, whereas the marginal social benefit curves are lower, separated by the spillover costs.
	- The marginal private costs (MPC) and marginal social cost (MSC) are represented by the supply curve, assuming no production externalities.
	- Since allocative efficiency occurs when MSB = MSC, but market equilibrium is dictated by MPB =MPC, the socially optimum quantity ($Q_{opt}$) is lower than the quantity produced by the market ($Q_m$). Therefore, there is overproduction of resources, welfare loss, allocative inefficiency, and hence market failure.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/709e85a4-af4a-418e-9af9-15fa3f95e160)

- As seen by the diagram below, the imposition of taxes on demerit goods shifts the demand supply upwards ($S \rightarrow S_{+tax}$), and so can correct the market failure by decreasing the quantity produced by the free market ($Q_m \rightarrow Q_{opt}$).
- Therefore, governments imposing taxes on demerit goods aim to reduce the quantity of the good provided by the market.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e3434ac8-23fb-444e-8856-48d133232171)

**Evaluation**  
- **Short-Run and Long-Run Effects**  
  - In the short run, consumers of demerit goods (e.g., cigarettes) have inelastic demand because of addiction or lack of immediate substitutes. Governments can earn substantial revenue, and consumption reductions may be modest.  
  - In the long run, demand can become more elastic as consumers adjust behavior or seek alternatives, reducing consumption further but also lowering government revenue.  

- **Real-world examples**  
  - In **Australia**, high tobacco taxes contributed over AUD 12 billion in government revenue in a recent year. Smoking prevalence dropped moderately in the short run (around 0.7 percentage points annually) due to strong addiction factors (inelastic demand). Over longer periods, public health campaigns and smoking bans contributed to more substantial declines, reflecting a shift toward more elastic demand.  
  - In **Mexico**, a sugary drinks tax introduced in 2014 led to a roughly 7.6% decline in purchases in the first year. The presence of alternative beverages made demand more elastic over time. Government revenue from the soda tax was initially significant but began to stabilize as consumers gradually shifted to substitutes.  

- **Conclusion**  
  - PED strongly influences how effectively an indirect tax reduces consumption of demerit goods and how much revenue is generated.  
  - Goods with inelastic demand ensure higher revenue but less immediate impact on consumption, while goods with elastic demand may show larger consumption decreases but lower long-term tax revenue.  
  - Policymakers often consider PED to balance the goals of raising revenue and discouraging harmful consumption.

questions.specification:
## Economic Policies and Developments in Liberia

Liberia, located in West Africa, is a low-income economy seeking to recover from years of civil conflict and health crises. Although the country’s real GDP growth edged up to 2.5% in 2021, it has lagged behind other countries in the region, with persistent poverty and underemployment. Recently, the government announced the Reconstruction and Diversification Initiative (RDI), intended to reduce the economy’s reliance on exporting raw rubber and iron ore. Under this plan, there is a stronger emphasis on agriculture, light manufacturing, and human capital development.

To stabilize domestic food prices, the Ministry of Commerce introduced a subsidy on staple rice in 2020. This subsidy aims to reduce the market price paid by consumers and maintain food security. Critics argue, however, that the subsidy may distort resource allocation and place a heavy burden on an already constrained national budget. The government also maintains a price floor for selected agricultural products to support local farmers. While these interventions have provided short-term relief for consumers and producers, there are concerns about their long-term fiscal sustainability.

Macroeconomic stability is a key priority for Liberia’s central bank, which conducts monetary policy using interest rate adjustments and open market operations. In 2022, the annual inflation rate reached 9%, down from 13% three years earlier, partly due to tighter monetary policies. Nonetheless, high unemployment remains a challenge, with youth unemployment estimated at 15%. There are calls for more expansionary measures to support formal job creation.

Under the RDI, the government has pledged to increase capital expenditures on infrastructure particularly rural roads, electricity grids, and water systems to ease the cost of doing business and attract foreign direct investment (FDI). Although net FDI inflows fell to US 250 million in 2021 (from US 300 million in 2019), officials are hopeful that improved infrastructure and reforms in the business environment will drive higher investment levels in the near future.

On the international front, Liberia’s main trading partners include the European Union, the United States, and other ECOWAS (Economic Community of West African States) nations. Iron ore and rubber exports account for over 45% of the country’s total export revenues. In line with the RDI, policymakers are negotiating additional trade agreements that reduce tariffs on processed agricultural goods, hoping to spur export diversification. However, some observers worry that increased competition from these trade deals might hurt local firms that cannot meet international standards.

Income distribution remains uneven: the Gini coefficient stands at approximately 0.38, reflecting moderate inequality, yet stark urban-rural divides persist. The government has expanded conditional cash transfer programs and vocational training to tackle poverty. Still, around 44% of the population lives below the national poverty line, underscoring the magnitude of the development challenge.

Although external debt is rising, the authorities argue that borrowing is essential to finance infrastructure critical for economic transformation. Public investment in health and education has risen by 5% annually since 2019. Proponents of this approach claim it will reduce longer-term reliance on raw commodity exports and transition the economy to higher-value production.

With Liberia’s fragile economic base, debates center around whether government intervention, including subsidies and price floors, truly fosters growth or creates distortions. Private sector representatives support the RDI’s focus on export diversification, yet caution against overreliance on external loans. As the country negotiates new trade agreements, continued improvements in human capital and infrastructure will likely be critical for Liberia’s sustained development.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Liberia (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                      | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|-------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)     | 3.10  | 3.05  | 3.20  | 3.40  |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)      | 1.4   | -3.0  | 2.5   | 3.0   |
| Inflation Rate (%)            | 13.0  | 11.5  | 10.0  | 9.0   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)         | 14.0  | 14.2  | 15.0  | 14.8  |
| Net FDI Inflows (US$ million) | 300   | 270   | 250   | 260   |

**Table 2: Sectoral Distribution & Targets Under the RDI**

| Sector                 | Current Share of GDP (%) | RDI Target (Share of GDP by 2025) (%) | Estimated Gov’t Investment (US$ million) |
|------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Rubber & Iron Ore      | 45                       | 35                                    | 100                                      |
| Agriculture            | 25                       | 30                                    | 150                                      |
| Light Manufacturing    | 10                       | 15                                    | 80                                       |
| Services (Tourism, etc.) | 20                    | 20                                    | 70                                       |

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the potential impact of the Reconstruction and Diversification Initiative (RDI) on Liberia’s growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Economic growth: An increase in real output (real GDP) over time.  
- Economic development: A broader concept involving improvements in standards of living, reduction in poverty, and enhanced access to education and health-care.  

**Range of Arguments:**

- Emphasis on Diversification  
  - The RDI aims to reduce dependence on rubber and iron ore, which together form 45% of export revenues. Greater emphasis on agriculture (targeting 30% of GDP) and light manufacturing (targeting 15% of GDP) may help stabilize export revenues and reduce vulnerability to commodity price fluctuations.  
  - In the long term, diversification could lead to more stable growth, but it requires significant investment in infrastructure, technology transfer, and human capital. If this investment is insufficient or mismanaged, the economy could struggle to move away from commodity exports.

- Infrastructure Investment and Productivity  
  - The government plans to expand rural roads, electricity grids, and water systems, potentially reducing production costs and attracting foreign direct investment. Net FDI has declined from US 300 million in 2019 to US 250 million in 2021, but better infrastructure could reverse this trend.  
  - In the short term, increased government spending on infrastructure may cause budget deficits and add to Liberia’s external debt. However, improved infrastructure can boost Liberia’s long-run growth (LRAS1 to LRAS2) potential by encouraging private sector expansion and employment opportunities.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e1a04055-8fa6-4ac0-b035-b26bf1ab981f)

- Impact on Government Finances and Sustainability  
  - Liberia’s public investment in health and education has grown by 5% annually since 2019, suggesting efforts to develop human capital. This improves the skills base necessary for diversification. However, external debt is increasing, prompting concerns about long-term fiscal sustainability.  
  - Borrowing to finance infrastructure could be beneficial if it generates returns that exceed borrowing costs. Yet there is a risk that high debt repayments may crowd out other vital public services.

- Balance Between Market Forces and Government Intervention  
  - Price interventions, such as subsidies and price floors for agricultural products, support farmers and consumers in the short run but may create market distortions. Over the long term, inefficient allocation of resources and strain on the budget might hamper development initiatives.  
  - A more targeted subsidy program might be necessary to protect vulnerable groups without placing excessive pressure on government finances.

- Export Competitiveness and Exchange Rate Effects  
  - Negotiating trade agreements to lower tariffs on processed goods may encourage industrial development and diversify exports. A rise in exports could strengthen the Liberian dollar. However, local firms may face intense competition if they cannot comply with international standards.  
  - If the currency appreciates strongly, export competitiveness for traditional commodities might decline, and more balanced policymaking will be required to mitigate these pressures.

- Human Capital Development  
  - The RDI involves supporting education and vocational training, especially since youth unemployment is at 15%. Investing in human capital is critical for improving productivity and fostering broad-based development.  
  - If training programs are well-designed and aligned with new industries, this can lead to higher employment, reduced inequality, and sustained economic growth.

**Conclusion and Evaluation:**

  - The RDI could stimulate long-term growth by broadening the economic base, upgrading infrastructure, and improving human capital. In the process, development indicators like income equality and poverty rates may improve, especially if growth is inclusive.  
  - However, the success of these reforms depends on prudent debt management, the ability to implement large-scale projects effectively, and balancing government intervention with market incentives. Overreliance on external borrowing and ongoing market distortions might limit the long-term benefits.  
- Overall, the RDI has the potential to boost Liberia’s economic growth and development by promoting diversification, improving infrastructure, and enhancing human capital. 
- Its success, however, largely depends on whether the country can manage borrowed funds responsibly, address structural challenges, and maintain a stable macroeconomic environment.

questions.specification:
Colombia is located in the northwestern region of South America. It is the world’s third-largest producer of coffee, accounting for around 10% of global supply. The Colombian National Coffee Growers Federation (FNC) estimates that approximately 500 000 families depend on coffee production for their livelihoods. In 2022, Colombia earned an estimated US $2.5 billion from coffee exports, representing about 8% of the country’s total export revenue.

Although the Colombian economy has shown signs of growth, income inequality remains a concern. With a Gini coefficient of about 0.50 in 2022, Colombia has one of the highest levels of income inequality in Latin America. The government is exploring new social welfare programs and potential reforms to its tax system to address these disparities.

Table 1 shows key macroeconomic indicators for Colombia between 2021 and 2022. It includes data on real GDP, population, income inequality, government spending, and tax revenue.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Colombia, 2021–2022

| Indicator                                    | 2021                 | 2022                 |
|---------------------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Real GDP (billion US$, constant 2015)       | 300                  | 330                  |
| Population (millions)                       | 49.5                 | 50.3                 |
| Gini coefficient                            | 0.51                 | 0.50                 |
| Official poverty rate (%)                   | 35                   | 34                   |
| Government spending (billion US$)           | 60                   | 63                   |
| Tax revenue (billion US$)                   | 52                   | 55                   |

Between January and July 2022, the price of Arabica coffee rose significantly, from US $1.20 per pound to US $1.50 per pound, partly due to strong global demand and weather-related supply constraints. Table 2 shows the changes in price, quantity demanded by the international market, and quantity supplied domestically in Colombia during this period (quantities are measured in millions of standard 60-kg bags of coffee).

Table 2: Price and Quantity Changes in the Colombian Coffee Market, Jan–Jul 2022

| Month    | Price (US$/lb) | Quantity Demanded (millions of 60-kg bags) | Quantity Supplied (millions of 60-kg bags) |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| January  | 1.20           | 5.0                                       | 4.0                                       |
| July     | 1.50           | 4.7                                       | 4.3                                       |

In 2022, Colombia’s inflation rate was approximately 4.5%. The Ministry of Finance is considering increasing government investment in the coffee sector to boost production capacity and generate additional employment. Economists estimate a Keynesian multiplier of 1.4 for new investments in the coffee sector, meaning that each dollar invested could generate an additional US $0.40 in national income.

Meanwhile, there is also debate around Colombia’s tax system. The current corporate tax rate stands at 31%, and personal income tax rates are progressive, peaking at 39% for top earners. The standard VAT rate is 19% on most goods and services, although some necessities have lower or zero VAT.

parts.content: Calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Colombian coffee between January and July 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(4.7 − 5.0) ÷ 5.0] × 100 = ( − 0.3 / 5.0 ) × 100 = −6%  
• Percentage change in price = [(1.50 − 1.20) ÷ 1.20] × 100 = (0.30 / 1.20) × 100 = 25%  
• PED = ( −6% ) / 25% = −0.24 (absolute value 0.24)

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Colombian government could implement to reduce income inequality while supporting the coffee sector.
parts.markscheme:
• Define income inequality as the unequal distribution of income among individuals or households, highlighted by Colombia’s Gini coefficient of 0.50.  
• Define a subsidy as financial assistance provided by the government to firms to lower their production costs and increase output.  
• Propose a targeted subsidy program for small- to medium-scale coffee producers that decreases production costs and encourages higher output to meet global demand, building on Colombia’s significant coffee export revenues of US \$2.5 billion.  
• Explain that subsidizing coffee farms can shift the supply curve to the right in the coffee market, leading to potentially lower prices, higher production, and increased incomes for coffee-growing families.  
• Describe how increased supply in a key export sector can generate export revenue, feed into higher GDP growth, and utilize the Keynesian multiplier of 1.4, so each US \$1 invested can add US \$0.40 in national income.  
• Highlight that increased incomes for coffee-related households could help reduce the official poverty rate (currently 34%) and narrow income inequality by delivering benefits directly to vulnerable groups dependent on coffee production.  
• Emphasize that a targeted government subsidy can work alongside a slightly more progressive direct tax structure (currently peaking at 39% for top earners) to redistribute gains from the coffee sector and fund social welfare programs.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/25b11567-83ee-4ad6-b083-3bbae1a2b751)
• Reference the Gini coefficient’s decline from 0.51 to 0.50, suggesting that a combination of direct support to farmers and progressive taxation can further reduce inequality.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6353a38d-bdfe-4209-a3b8-f1e46440a709)
• Note that ensuring government spending remains sustainable at 63 billion US\$ compared with a 55 billion US\$ tax revenue requires careful budgeting to avoid excessive deficits.  
• Conclude that targeted subsidies for coffee producers, combined with complementary tax reforms, can foster inclusive economic growth, support a key export sector, and continue the downward trend in Colombia’s Gini coefficient.

questions.specification:
## Nigeria’s Economic Diversification and Policy Reforms

Nigeria, located in West Africa, is Africa’s largest economy by gross domestic product (GDP). Despite its vast oil reserves, persistent macroeconomic challenges such as volatile global oil prices, currency instability, and inflation have prompted the government to adopt policies beyond the oil sector. In an effort to ensure greater economic resilience, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) has recently broadened its monetary policy measures (mentioned in paragraph 3) to stabilize the naira and control inflation, which reached 17% in 2021 from 15% the previous year.

Historically, Nigeria employed import-substitution policies (introduced in paragraph 2) to reduce reliance on imported goods and to support domestic industries. However, these measures often led to higher production costs and limited competitiveness in foreign markets. As a result, policymakers are now focusing on diversifying exports, notably integrating Nigeria more fully into the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) to increase non-oil trade. Exports of agricultural produce, such as cocoa and sesame seeds, have indeed grown by over 20% during the past three years, supporting rural incomes.

Amid these structural shifts, the CBN has continued to use monetary policy tools to manage the money supply and influence interest rates. The government, in collaboration with the Bank of Industry, has launched credit schemes to encourage small and medium-sized enterprises in technology and manufacturing, hoping to foster domestic value addition. While early indicators show promise, entrepreneurs report challenges like erratic electricity supply and limited infrastructure, constraining large-scale industrial growth.

Nigeria’s population estimated at 210 million in 2022 continues to grow rapidly and places pressure on public services. Infrastructure developments, including a major highway project linking Lagos and Ibadan, aim to reduce transportation bottlenecks that raise firms’ costs. Environmental concerns have emerged in areas centered on gas flaring and deforestation. Economists suggest that addressing these negative externalities could promote more sustainable growth in the long term.

The exchange rate for the naira faced volatility when oil prices declined sharply in 2020. In response, the CBN used foreign exchange reserves to defend the currency, though parallel market rates diverged significantly. Some exporters argue that a more flexible exchange rate would make Nigerian goods more competitive abroad. On the other hand, importers worry about rising costs of essential inputs, including pharmaceuticals and capital equipment.

As the government pursues a diversification strategy, social indicators remain mixed. While nominal GDP per capita rose from US 2,000 in 2018 to approximately US 2,200 in 2021, income inequality persists, with the Gini coefficient hovering around 0.35. Efforts to improve economic inclusion include removing certain fuel subsidies and reallocating funds to social welfare. Critics, however, argue that cutting fuel subsidies prematurely may raise transport and production costs, thus harming vulnerable households.

Looking ahead, the government aims to maintain macroeconomic stability while promoting human capital development. Programs designed to improve nutrition and education may boost labor productivity, especially in the agricultural sector. With Nigeria’s entrance into new trade agreements and strides in improving the business environment for investors, economists anticipate gradual shifts away from oil dependency. Nonetheless, the success of these reforms largely depends on effective policy coordination, infrastructural improvements, and the engagement of private sector stakeholders.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Nigeria (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                             | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|---------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)            | 448   | 420   | 440   | 462   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)             | 2.2   | -1.8  | 3.0   | 2.7   |
| Inflation Rate (%)                   | 11.4  | 15.0  | 17.0  | 16.5  |
| Official Exchange Rate (NGN per US$) | 306   | 361   | 416   | 430   |
| Oil Exports (% of total exports)     | 88    | 87    | 82    | 80    |

**Table 2: Development and Social Indicators for Nigeria**

|----------------------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Population (million)                               | 200    | 205    | 210    | 215    |
| Gini Coefficient                                   | 0.35   | 0.35   | 0.35   | 0.38   |
| Fuel Subsidy (US$ billion)                         | 1.5    | 1.8    | 2.2    | 2.0    |
| Non-oil Exports (US$ billion)                      | 12.0   | 10.5   | 12.6   | 13.1   |

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain how gas flaring in Nigeria’s oil sector (paragraph 4) might affect social welfare.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/013728d0-10cc-45c9-8043-c7d71cefb9e0)

- Gas flaring creates a negative externality because it imposes social costs such as environmental damage and health risks not fully borne by oil producers.
-  In a standard negative externalities diagram, the marginal social cost (MSC) is above the marginal private cost (MPC), so the market equilibrium output is higher than the socially optimal level(Qm>Qopt)
-  This leads to a welfare loss because society bears extra costs (pollution, resource depletion), reducing overall societal well-being. 

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how the Central Bank of Nigeria’s intervention in the foreign exchange market (paragraph 5) might influence the naira’s value.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ee89c228-d33e-404c-b5d9-4338bc483762)

- When the Central Bank defends the naira using foreign currency reserves, it effectively increases demand for naira in the foreign exchange market. 
- An exchange rate diagram would show a rightward shift in the demand curve for naira (D1 shift to D2), causing the currency’s value to rise or stabilize instead of depreciating further. 
- This intervention can maintain a stronger official exchange rate(e2>e1), although the parallel market rate may still diverge.

Better roads, power supply, and communications systems reduce production costs and enhance efficiency, raising potential output. 
- An AD/AS diagram would show higher aggregate demand initially (due to increased government expenditure) and a potential rightward shift in LRAS as productivity improves, leading to higher real output in the long term.

parts.content: Using information from the text/data (especially Table 2) and your knowledge of economics, discuss the potential impact of reducing fuel subsidies while promoting non-oil exports on Nigeria’s economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Fuel subsidy: A payment by the government to keep fuel prices below market equilibrium, often aiming to reduce costs for consumers and producers.  
- Economic growth: An increase in real output (real GDP) over time.  
- Economic development: Improvements in standards of living, reductions in poverty, and enhanced access to education and health-care.  

**Range of Arguments:**

Argument 1: Impact on Government Finances and Possible Reallocation  
- Reducing fuel subsidies frees government funds (from US 2.2 billion to US 2.0 billion in Table 2) that could be diverted to social welfare or infrastructure.  
- This reallocation may raise long-term productivity, supporting growth and broader development goals. This growth is indicated as a rightward shift of LRAS curve, from LRAS1 to LRAS2.  

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b8b2c8e4-6a05-4579-97a0-523197acf87f)

- However, higher fuel prices might raise firms’ transportation and production costs, affecting competitiveness and household living standards.  


Argument 2: Effects on Aggregate Demand and Real Incomes  
- Cutting fuel subsidies raises costs for consumers and firms, potentially dampening consumption and short-term growth if disposable income falls.  
- Households with lower incomes may be disproportionately affected, risking higher inequality, especially given the Gini coefficient’s rise to 0.38 in 2022.  
- If government support is redirected effectively into programs that increase aggregate demand (e.g., education, health-care), the negative impact on real incomes may be mitigated over time.  

Argument 3: Promoting Non-oil Exports and Diversification  
- Non-oil exports grew to US$13.1 billion in 2022, suggesting an expanding base if supported by further measures like better infrastructure and credit schemes.  
- Diversification reduces Nigeria’s reliance on volatile oil markets and benefits from initiatives like AfCFTA to access broader export markets.  
- Export expansion can boost job creation and incomes, but continuous power supply and stable regulatory conditions are vital for attracting foreign direct investment (rising to US$5.0 billion in Table 2).  

Argument 4: Long-term Development and Microeconomic Effects  
- Reduced subsidies can lower fiscal deficits and improve macroeconomic stability, encouraging business confidence.  
- More efficient resource allocation can address negative externalities from fossil fuels and support sustainable industrialization.  
- However, abrupt subsidy removal might trigger social unrest if not accompanied by transitional support for vulnerable groups, undermining development targets.  

**Conclusion:**

- Reducing fuel subsidies while promoting non-oil exports can enhance long-term growth and development by reallocating resources to more productive sectors and reducing dependence on oil.  
- Success depends on the timing of subsidy cuts, targeted support to reduce inequality, and continued investment in infrastructure to improve competitiveness.  
- The overall impact is likely positive if accompanied by effective policy coordination, but short-term costs to consumers and firms highlight the need for gradual reforms and robust social safety nets.

questions.specification:
## TRADE NEGOTIATIONS BETWEEN CANADA AND VIETNAM

Canada is a high-income economy in North America with a focus on advanced manufacturing, services, and natural resources. Vietnam, located in Southeast Asia, has experienced rapid industrialization and a growing consumer market. Both countries are pursuing a new agreement to strengthen their economic ties. This proposed deal aims to enhance foreign direct investment, reduce trade barriers, and deepen cooperation in areas such as infrastructure, technology, and agricultural products. As a result, bilateral trade between the two countries is projected to increase by 20% annually over the next five years.

Canada’s government officials highlight the need to address protectionism through tariff reductions and the elimination of non-tariff barriers. They plan to remove an existing quota on imported Vietnamese textiles, while Vietnam seeks to cut tariffs on Canadian dairy and wheat. In 2022, Canadian exports to Vietnam totaled US$15.2 billion, an 18% increase from the previous year. By removing strict regulations and simplifying customs procedures, both nations hope to see further growth in their external trade.

However, inflationary pressures have recently surfaced in both economies. Canada’s inflation rate reached 4.8% in 2022, driven by rising energy and food costs, prompting the Bank of Canada to raise interest rates. Similarly, Vietnam saw inflation of 3.9%, attributed to increasing demand for imported raw materials and consumer goods. Higher interest rates often slow aggregate demand, but policymakers in Canada believe that improved trade relations with Vietnam could help stabilize prices by increasing supply options and competition in the domestic market.

On the other hand, Vietnam remains concerned about a potential balance of payments deficit if imports outpace exports once tariffs are lowered. Recent figures show that Vietnam’s merchandise trade surplus narrowed from US$6.1 billion in 2021 to US$4.5 billion in 2022. Part of this change is explained by rising imports of Canadian machinery for Vietnam’s manufacturing sector. Both governments are considering targeted subsidy programs to encourage domestic firms to move up the value chain, particularly in high-tech and environmentally friendly products.

In the short run, labor markets in both countries are expected to be affected by structural changes. While Canada may see an increase in demand for specialized technical workers, Vietnam could face challenges if under-resourced industries fail to compete with cheaper Canadian imports. Some Vietnamese business leaders have called for cautious measures to protect strategic industries that generate significant employment. Nevertheless, many economists argue that such protective measures may distort the market and slow long-term growth.

Environmental concerns also feature prominently in the negotiations. Canada has set a goal of reducing carbon emissions by 40% from 2005 levels by 2030, while Vietnam is pursuing renewable energy initiatives to diversify its power generation. The proposed deal includes provisions for technology sharing in clean energy, aiming to reduce fossil fuel dependency. Government representatives in both countries emphasize that green technology investment could create new opportunities for job growth and export diversification.

Table 1 provides selected macroeconomic data for Canada and Vietnam, illustrating the steady expansion in real GDP for both countries. Table 2 highlights trade figures, including each nation’s principal exports, which underscore the complementarity of their industries. Policymakers on both sides hope that increased cooperation will stimulate not only trade volumes but also innovation and sustainable development, especially in agriculture, manufacturing, and services.

Despite optimism from many sectors, critics warn that the agreement must carefully balance growth with socioeconomic equity. Consumer advocates in Canada are concerned about potential job losses in industries facing new competition, while Vietnamese farmers worry about cheaper imports from Canada eroding local market share. If the trade deal is ratified, officials will need to monitor its effects on inflation, exchange rates, and income distribution carefully. Both governments have signaled willingness to use fiscal and monetary tools to mitigate any adverse impacts, acknowledging that global trade dynamics often require timely policy intervention.

**TABLE 1: SELECTED MACROECONOMIC DATA (2022)**

| Indicator                         | Canada            | Vietnam           |
|----------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Real GDP (US$ billions)          | 1 890            | 366               |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)         | 3.4              | 6.5               |
| Inflation Rate (%)               | 4.8              | 3.9               |
| Unemployment Rate (%)            | 5.9              | 2.2               |
| Interest Rate (%)                | 3.5              | 4.0               |

**TABLE 2: TRADE DATA BETWEEN CANADA AND VIETNAM**

| Trade Indicator                    | Canada → Vietnam | Vietnam → Canada |
|-----------------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Annual Export Value (US$ billion) | 15.2 (↑18%)      | 9.7 (↑12%)       |
| Main Export Products              | Machinery, Dairy, Wheat | Textiles, Electronics, Seafood |
| FDI Outflows (US$ billion)        | 2.5             | 1.8              |
| Tariff Rates on Key Goods (%)     | 10              | 12               |
| Quotas                            | Dairy Quota Removed | Textiles Quota Removed |

parts.content: Define the term bilateral trade indicated in bold in the text (Paragraph 1).
parts.markscheme: Bilateral trade is the exchange of goods and services between two countries free from substantial trade barriers, ensuring mutual benefits and increased economic cooperation.

parts.content: Define the term quota indicated in bold in the text (Paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: A quota is a physical limit set by a country on the quantity or value of certain imported goods over a specific period, aiming to protect domestic industries or manage trade flows.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate Canada’s approximate real GDP per capita, assuming a population of 38 million.
parts.markscheme:
Step-by-step calculation for Canada’s real GDP per capita:  
- Canada’s real GDP (2022) = US$1 890 billion. 
- Population = 38 million.  
- Real GDP per capita = 1 890 billion ÷ 38 million = US$49 737 approximately.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how rising import prices of raw materials (Paragraph 3) could affect inflation and real output in Vietnam.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/50f5729f-ff66-45c7-a39b-e6ba16563dc6)

- Rising import prices of raw materials increase production costs, causing short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) to shift to the left (SRAS1 to SRAS2).  
- A leftward shift of SRAS leads to cost-push inflation (a higher price level) and lower real output (Pl1 to Pl2).  
- This effect is in line with the inflationary pressures Vietnam faces, with limited ability to expand SRAS if the raw material cost surge continues.  
- Higher prices can erode purchasing power and potentially slow down economic growth if no offsetting policies are introduced.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how concerns over Vietnam’s narrowing trade surplus (Paragraph 4) might influence the value of the Vietnamese dong.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9781e205-68d1-4d88-8b1c-2616ffc64213)

- A shrinking trade surplus indicates relatively lower net export earnings, reducing the demand for the Vietnamese dong on foreign exchange markets.  
- Lower demand or increased supply of dong (to pay for more imports) places downward pressure on the currency’s value (e1 to e2).  
- On the exchange rate diagram, this is typically shown by a shift of the demand curve (D1 to D2) for dong to the left or the supply curve to the right, leading to a depreciation of the currency.  
- The depreciation could stimulate exports by making them cheaper but may also raise the cost of imports, contributing further to inflationary pressures.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an international trade diagram, explain how removing the quota on textiles (Paragraph 2) may affect consumer surplus and producer surplus in Canada.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2549e4af-b771-4553-a1f0-77d49ce1d464)

- Removing the quota allows a greater quantity of cheaper Vietnamese textiles to enter the Canadian market.  
- Consumer surplus in Canada increases because consumers can access a larger supply at potentially lower prices.  
- Domestic producers in Canada may face increased competition and lose some producer surplus due to downward pressure on prices and reduced market share.  
- Overall, the market moves closer to a free-trade equilibrium, with higher overall economic welfare but potential losses for domestic textile firms.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain the impact on society due to the possible reduction in fossil fuel dependency through the “clean energy” provisions in the proposed agreement (Paragraph 6).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/298dac73-c63c-457f-b2b5-ae15ba7614ba)

- Fossil fuel consumption is associated with negative externalities, such as pollution and greenhouse gases.  
- A shift toward green technology reduces the negative external cost, causing the socially optimal output to be closer to the private equilibrium level.  
- In a negative production externality diagram, a consumption of fossils fulls would lead to the MSC curve shiftting left (S1 to S2) away from the MPC curve.
- Overall welfare rises as society experiences fewer pollutant effects, and firms may internalize some of the external costs through cleaner production methods.  
- Lower negative externalities lead to improved social wellbeing, though transitional costs might occur as industries adjust to cleaner energy sources.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of the proposed Canada–Vietnam trade agreement on long-term economic growth and development in both countries.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**  

- Economic growth: an increase in real output or real GDP over time.  
- Economic development: broad-based improvements in living standards, often measured by increases in income, education, health, and more equitable distribution of wealth.  
- Trade agreement: an arrangement between countries to improve trade by removing or reducing barriers such as tariffs, quotas, and non-tariff measures.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Argument 1: Increased bilateral trade and FDI  
  - Both countries benefit from higher exports: Canada’s dairy, wheat, and machinery; Vietnam’s textiles, electronics, and seafood.  
  - According to Table 2, Canada’s exports to Vietnam are already at US$15.2 billion, growing by 18%, indicating a demand for Canadian goods in Vietnam.  
  - Greater FDI inflows can spur the development of new industries, technology transfer, and create jobs.  
  - However, excessive reliance on foreign demand might make each economy vulnerable to external shocks.  

- Argument 2: Effects on Inflation and Price Stability  
  - Canada’s officials see potential benefits in stabilizing prices through expanded supply options, but importing cheaper goods may conflict with domestic producers’ interests.  
  - Vietnam’s inflation (3.9%) could rise further if tariffs on Canadian imports are lowered without productivity gains, as well as from currency adjustments.  
  - Balanced trade policies could mitigate excessive inflation by diversifying supply chains and ensuring competition.

- Argument 3: Structural Changes and Employment  
  - Canada could see more high-skilled jobs in technology and services if demand for specialized technical workers rises (Paragraph 5).  
  - Vietnam may face short-term losses in strategic industries (like textiles) if they cannot compete on price or quality.  
  - Over time, Vietnam could move up the value chain via targeted subsidies to high-tech and green industries, enhancing productivity.  
  - Structural change fosters long-run economic growth, but retraining and relocation programs might be essential to reduce unemployment risks.

- Argument 4: Effects on Balance of Payments and Exchange Rates  
  - Vietnam’s narrowing surplus (from US$6.1b to US$4.5b) may lead to currency depreciation, stimulating exports but raising import costs.  
  - Canada might see improvements in its current account if its goods remain competitive, boosting investment and growth.  
  - Potential volatility in currency markets (Paragraph 4) requires national policymakers to consider how to maintain external stability.

- Argument 5: Environmental and Sustainable Development  
  - Canada and Vietnam’s commitment to reducing carbon emissions (Paragraph 6) can foster greener technology and renewable energy.  
  - Collaboration may result in lower long-term economic costs of climate change, creating new green sectors and jobs.  
  - Initial implementation costs could be significant, but the long-term benefits include cleaner environments and diversified energy sources.
  - The use of a positive production externality shows the shift of the supply curve outwards (Sm1 to Sm2) bringing the MPC curve back to the MSC curve (MPC1 to MSC=MPC2).

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/238f0d92-45ac-40c7-8afd-dab7e7f06798)

- Argument 6: Income Distribution and Equity Concerns  
  - Critics caution about job losses in certain sectors and the possibility of rural communities in Vietnam or small-scale producers in Canada losing out.  
  - Governments may need safety nets, such as retraining, subsidies, or social programs, to ensure broader development.  
  - Trade-driven growth might raise overall income, but equitable distribution requires policy intervention (Paragraph 8).

**Conclusion and Evaluation:** 

- Strengths:  
  - The agreement can increase trade flows, foster technological advancement, and spur long-run growth.  
  - Encouraging green industries and innovation may lead to sustainable development.  
- Limitations:  
  - Short-term structural disruptions in labor markets might harm certain industries.  
  - Inflationary pressures and balance of payments concerns may undermine some of the potential gains.  
  - Success depends on implementing policies that protect vulnerable groups, control inflation, and guide investment toward sustainable sectors.  
- Overall, if carefully managed with complementary policies (e.g., targeted subsidies, retraining programs, and stable macroeconomic policy), the proposed deal could significantly boost long-term economic growth and development in both Canada and Vietnam, enhancing welfare and promoting sustainable practices.

questions.specification:
Brazil is the largest economy in Latin America, with a population of approximately 214 million people. Real GDP growth in 2022 was reported at 2.9%, partly driven by the agricultural sector and commodity exports. Income inequality remains high, reflected in a Gini coefficient of around 0.53. Despite its diversified economy, Brazil relies heavily on primary commodities such as coffee, soybeans, and iron ore for export revenue.

Coffee is a major export for Brazil and has historically been susceptible to fluctuations in global demand and changes in weather patterns. In 2022, unfavorable climate conditions in certain coffee-producing regions contributed to a lower-than-expected harvest, pushing up coffee prices internationally. Meanwhile, the Brazilian real (BRL) has undergone phases of depreciation against the US dollar over the past few years, affecting both export competitiveness and the cost of imported inputs.

Beyond agriculture, the Brazilian government faces the challenge of ensuring inclusive growth. Income inequality remains persistent, with approximately 21% of the population living below the national poverty line. While the government collects both direct and indirect taxes, efforts to reform the tax system continue, aimed at improving the efficiency of tax collection and reducing the burden on lower-income households.

Below are selected data tables with information on Brazil’s labor market, macroeconomic indicators, and coffee market conditions. You will use these data as well as the text above to answer the questions in Part (a) and Part (b).

**Table 1: Basic labor market data for Brazil (2022)**

| Population (millions) | Labor Force (millions) | Employed (millions) | Unemployed (millions) |
|-----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| 214                   | 108                    | 99                  | 9                     |

**Table 2: Selected macroeconomic indicators for Brazil**

| Year | Nominal GDP (billion US$) | GDP Deflator (Index, base year = 2021) |
|------|---------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 2021 | 1620                      | 1.00                                   |
| 2022 | 1780                      | 1.05                                   |

**Table 3: Coffee market data for Brazil (2022)**

| Price of coffee (US$/lb) | Quantity demanded (thousand tonnes) |
|--------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 2.80                     | 2250                                 |
| 3.20                     | 2100                                 |

Additional information:
- One tonne of coffee ≈ 2,204.6 lbs (pounds).  
- Approximately 70% of Brazil’s coffee production is exported.  
- The Gini coefficient for Brazil stands at 0.53.  
- The government collects both direct taxes (on personal and corporate income) and indirect taxes (such as ICMS on goods and services).

parts.content: Explain why an appreciation of the Brazilian real (BRL) might lead to a reduction in Brazil’s net exports of coffee.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/555ef7fe-8ed8-4e8f-a9a3-737b4995df2e)

- An appreciation increases the value of the BRL, making Brazilian coffee more expensive for international buyers.  
- Higher export prices reduce the quantity demanded of Brazilian coffee abroad, lowering export revenue.  
- At the same time, foreign goods become relatively cheaper in Brazil, increasing imports.  
- As a result, net exports (exports minus imports) of coffee decline because export revenue decreases while imports become more attractive.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the approximate inflation rate in Brazil between 2021 and 2022. Show your working.
Using the text above, explain how a rise in coffee exports might affect the current account of Brazil’s balance of payments.
parts.markscheme:
- Coffee exports represent a main source of export revenue, accounting for a significant portion of the goods section of the current account.  
- A rise in coffee exports increases export revenue, potentially helping to improve Brazil’s current account balance if imports remain unchanged.  
- Higher export earnings could offset the cost of imported inputs, especially given fluctuations in the Brazilian real.  
- This impact is particularly relevant since approximately 70% of Brazil’s coffee production is sold abroad, making the nation’s current account sensitive to changes in coffee export volumes.

parts.content: Using the data and your knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the Brazilian government could implement to reduce income inequality. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Define key terms related to income distribution, such as “income inequality” and “progressive taxation,” since Brazil has a Gini coefficient of 0.53, reflecting high inequality.  
- Propose the introduction of a more progressive tax system, where higher-income groups face increasing marginal tax rates, while lowering the reliance on indirect taxes that disproportionately affect lower-income households.  
- Clarify that reducing regressive taxation (like the ICMS on basic necessities) helps improve disposable incomes for the 21% of Brazilians living below the poverty line, lowering the Gini coefficient over time.  
- Highlight that progressive taxation can fund better-quality public services (health care, education, infrastructure) and directly address inequality, in line with Brazil’s objective of inclusive growth.  
- A more progressive tax system increases disposable income for low-income households, boosting consumption and causing aggregate demand to shift right (AD1 -> AD2). As a result, real output increases from Y to Yp and the price level rises from Pl1 to Pl2. The diagram shows how redistributive fiscal policy can reduce inequality while supporting short-run economic growth.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/20057930-7d3e-43d0-b1c6-b7758f5284cc)

- Note that although raising taxes on higher earners might potentially create disincentives to invest, the positive externalities from improved human capital and reduced poverty could outweigh these drawbacks.  
- Emphasize that, according to the text, large segments of the population remain at risk of poverty, so a policy focusing on equitable distribution and increased government revenue for social spending can lead to reduced inequality.  
- Argue that a progressive tax system requires careful implementation and enforcement to minimize tax evasion and maintain efficiency in tax collection, aligning with ongoing government efforts to reform Brazil’s tax structure.  
- Conclude that this policy could narrow income disparities, foster social stability, and potentially boost long-term economic growth by increasing the purchasing power of lower-income groups.

questions.specification:
## Economic and Development Challenges in Hungary

Hungary, located in Central Europe, has experienced notable economic fluctuations and structural changes over the past decade. Historically recognized for its manufacturing and strong ties to Western European markets, the country joined the European Union in 2004, facilitating trade liberalization and boosting foreign direct investment (FDI). However, rapid policy shifts, rising energy costs, and a global pandemic have tested Hungary’s resilience, resulting in a mixed record on economic growth and development.

In the period 2019–2021, Hungary’s real GDP growth averaged about 3.5%. A rebound in the automotive sector one of the main pillars of Hungarian manufacturing helped the economy recover after pandemic-related shutdowns. Nonetheless, in 2022, inflation surged to 14.5% annually, driven by rising energy and food prices, putting pressure on the purchasing power of consumers. At the same time, government spending on energy subsidies ballooned, aiming to shield households from full market prices, though critics argue that high-income earners receive a disproportionate share of these benefits. The Hungarian forint (HUF) has also depreciated against major currencies; in mid-2023, the exchange rate averaged 405 HUF per euro, up from 320 HUF per euro in 2019.

Unemployment stood at 5.6% in 2022, slightly above pre-pandemic levels. While labor shortages persist in certain skilled manufacturing roles, pockets of high unemployment endure in rural areas, exacerbating regional inequality. Many policymakers are concerned about the magnitude of cyclical poverty approximately 11% of Hungarians live below the national poverty line, with a higher incidence in eastern provinces. Additionally, official estimates suggest that 7% of the labor force remains employed in the informal sector, resulting in underreported incomes and limited tax revenues for the government.

The Hungarian government has pursued a combination of stimulus measures and reforms to target economic imbalances. One initiative is the Hungarian Development Initiative (HDI), which prioritizes education, infrastructure, and technology upgrades, aiming to enhance productivity and reduce regional disparities. Critics point out that corruption concerns and preferential contracts undermine the efficiency of public investments. On the environmental front, Hungary has worked to diversify its energy mix by investing in renewables, though fossil fuels still dominate electricity generation. High reliance on imported natural gas remains a concern for external vulnerability.

Policy debates also center on trade. Hungary benefits from no tariffs with its EU partners, and the automotive component trade with Germany remains vital. Yet, the government has been slow to reduce non-tariff barriers in areas like product certification. FDI inflows have stabilized recently, particularly in the logistics and services sectors, although analysts caution that geopolitical uncertainties could limit long-term investor sentiment.

Addressing poverty and inequality is a core social priority. Official data show that the Gini coefficient improved modestly from 0.31 in 2019 to 0.29 in 2022, although some economists question the accuracy of these figures, citing the extensive informal sector. Stakeholders emphasize that without consistent investment in vocational training and social protection, poverty may become entrenched in underdeveloped regions, perpetuating a poverty cycle.

At present, the government is caught between controlling inflation, supporting small and medium-sized enterprises, and managing public debt levels that reached 77% of GDP in 2022. The Central Bank of Hungary has intermittently intervened in foreign exchange markets to stabilize the currency. Observers note that policy coordination between monetary and fiscal authorities remains uneven, complicating the fight against rising consumer prices.

Looking ahead, the balance of risks and opportunities hinges on coherent policy implementation and structural reforms. Economic analysts urge Hungary to upgrade its industrial base, invest in human capital to foster innovation, and gradually phase out non-targeted subsidies. Ultimately, Hungary’s ability to transition toward more inclusive and sustainable growth will depend on the success of its development initiatives, the resilience of external demand, and the consistent enforcement of transparent governance practices.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Hungary (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                          | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
|------------------------------------|------|------|------|------|
| Nominal GDP (HUF trillion)         | 47.2 | 48.1 | 51.0 | 57.8 |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)           | 4.5  | -4.7 | 5.3  | 3.0  |
| Inflation Rate (%)                 | 3.3  | 3.7  | 5.2  | 14.5 |
| Unemployment Rate (%)              | 3.5  | 4.9  | 4.2  | 5.6  |
| Exchange Rate (HUF per Euro)       | 320  | 355  | 370  | 405  |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)            | 68   | 73   | 75   | 77   |

**Table 2: Development Indicators and Poverty Data for Hungary**

| Indicator                                                   | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |
|-------------------------------------------------------------|------|------|------|------|
| Gini Coefficient                                            | 0.31 | 0.30 | 0.30 | 0.29 |
| Poverty Rate (% of population below national poverty line)  | 10.0 | 10.5 | 10.8 | 11.0 |
| Informal Employment (% of total labor force)                | 6.5  | 6.8  | 7.0  | 7.0  |
| FDI Inflows (EUR billion)                                   | 3.2  | 2.4  | 2.9  | 3.0  |
| HDI Rank (out of 191 countries)                             | 40   | 42   | 42   | 43   |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage increase in Hungary’s nominal GDP from 2019 to 2022 (show your working).
parts.markscheme:
Percentage increase in Hungary’s nominal GDP from 2019 to 2022 (showing working):  
- Initial nominal GDP in 2019 = HUF 47.2 trillion  
- Final nominal GDP in 2022 = HUF 57.8 trillion  
- Change = 57.8 – 47.2 = HUF 10.6 trillion  
- Percentage increase = (10.6 / 47.2) × 100 ≈ 22.46% (approximately 22.5%)

parts.content: Using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, explain how investments under the Hungarian Development Initiative (HDI) could affect Hungary’s capacity to produce goods and services in the long run. (paragraph 4)
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/20617879-9fe0-40b9-b268-b2782e0b8094)

- Investments under the Hungarian Development Initiative (HDI), such as improved infrastructure, technology, and education, can expand the economy’s productive capacity over time. 
- On a PPC diagram, the curve shifts outward (PPC1 to PPC2), reflecting a higher potential output of both capital and consumer goods. Better infrastructure and training raise labor productivity, enabling firms to produce more goods and services.
- In the long run, this can lead to sustained growth by increasing both the quantity and quality of resources available.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how persistent depreciation of the Hungarian forint might influence Hungary’s net exports.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/6de78629-5949-4c85-aba4-53543b0332c1)

- Persistent depreciation of the Hungarian forint makes Hungarian exports relatively cheaper and imports more expensive.
-  In a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, an increase in supply of the currency or a decrease in demand (D1 to D2) for it can shift the currency’s equilibrium to a lower value. 
- The relatively cheaper exports may boost export revenue (assuming demand is price elastic), while expensive imports could reduce import expenditure, potentially improving net exports. However, higher import costs may also lead to inflationary pressures if imported inputs become costlier.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain the change in Hungary’s income inequality over the period 2019–2022, referring to the Gini coefficient data in Table 2.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7c35b57c-19e2-4484-b8e6-4e8f180909d9)

- A Lorenz curve diagrams the cumulative share of income against the cumulative share of the population. The Gini coefficient in Hungary decreased from 0.31 (2019) to 0.29 (2022), suggesting a more equal income distribution. 
- On the Lorenz curve, this would be illustrated by the curve moving closer to the line of equality, indicating less inequality over that period. Although the shift may be modest, it reflects a narrower gap between the highest and lowest income groups.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain why pockets of high unemployment and low skill levels in certain regions of Hungary may perpetuate cyclical poverty.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f749dcb9-0beb-4b67-877f-cd2a71f416af)

- High unemployment and low skill levels in certain regions can perpetuate a cycle of poverty.
-  A poverty cycle diagram shows that low skills and high unemployment limit earning potential, reducing income and thus limiting access to education and opportunities. This restricted access then reduces future human capital development, perpetuating the lack of skills. 
- The lack of investment in those regions further exacerbates poverty, creating intergenerational transmission of poor economic prospects.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text, Table 2, and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the extent to which Hungary’s non-targeted subsidies and development strategies are likely to reduce poverty and foster sustainable economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

- Sustainable economic growth: growth fulfilling present needs without compromising future generations.  
- Subsidies: financial assistance granted by the government to support certain sectors or consumers.  
- Poverty: a situation where individuals lack the income necessary to satisfy basic needs.

**Range of Arguments:**

- Argument 1: Non-targeted subsidies can protect households from inflation.  
  - Rising energy costs have driven inflation to 14.5%. Government energy subsidies shield households by lowering their effective energy bills, maintaining purchasing power and preventing a severe drop in living standards.  
  - This may ease immediate hardship for low-income citizens who spend a large proportion of their income on essentials, helping them avoid deeper poverty.  
  - However, the text notes that richer households receive a disproportionate share of these subsidies, which can be regressive and limit their impact on true poverty reduction.

- Argument 2: Non-targeted subsidies can lead to inefficiency and increased public debt.  
  - Large-scale subsidies can strain public finances; data show public debt has risen to 77% of GDP.  
  - Subsidies may also distort market signals and reduce the incentive to invest in energy efficiency or alternative sources of energy.  
  - This could hinder long-term sustainability if government funds are diverted away from development initiatives.

- Argument 3: Development strategies such as the Hungarian Development Initiative (HDI) can expand long-term productive capacity.  
  - According to the text, HDI invests in education, infrastructure, and technology. These investments shift out the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS1 to LRAS2) and promote human capital growth.  
  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/85d73c13-7bc1-4fa2-926f-b822ecd8722e)
	
  - Planned vocational training reduces skills mismatches, which helps address regional unemployment. Investing in infrastructure improves logistics, facilitating trade and FDI.  
  - Over time, higher productivity and a more skilled workforce can enable more inclusive growth, potentially lifting people out of poverty on a sustained basis.

- Argument 4: Corruption and inefficiency may undermine HDI effectiveness.  
  - The text highlights concerns about preferential contracts and lack of transparency, implying that development spending might not reach the most critical sectors or populations.  
  - Corruption can lead to misallocation of funds and undermine trust and effectiveness of any development program, reducing its impact on sustainable growth and poverty reduction.

- Argument 5: Persistent depreciation of the currency influences external competitiveness.  
  - A weaker forint makes Hungary’s exports cheaper. If export revenue rises, higher growth may provide more resources for poverty alleviation.  
  - However, depreciation also raises import prices, which can contribute to inflation. If inflation remains high, it erodes real incomes and can offset gains from subsidies and other growth initiatives.

- Argument 6: Potential benefits for poverty reduction might be offset by rising inequality in certain regions.  
  - The Gini coefficient improved slightly (0.31 to 0.29), but there is still notable rural poverty (11% below poverty line) and a 7% informal sector.  
  - Unless investment is targeted regionally to improve local conditions (e.g., education, business support), non-targeted subsidies will not break the regional poverty cycle.

**Conclusion:**  
- Non-targeted subsidies offer short-term relief but risk inefficiency, high fiscal costs, and regressive outcomes if not well-targeted.  
- Long-term poverty reduction and sustainable growth hinge more on effective investments in human capital, transparent governance, and infrastructure (HDI) than broad-based subsidies.  
- Appropriately managed development strategies, coupled with better targeting of assistance, are more likely to reduce poverty while enabling sustainable growth. If corruption is tackled and the subsidies are reformed to target the most vulnerable groups, positive and enduring effects are more likely.

questions.specification:
## Brazil’s Economy in Transition

Brazil, the largest country in Latin America, has undergone significant economic and social transformations in recent years. In 2020, Brazil’s real GDP contracted by 4.1% due to disruptions in global trade and domestic demand. However, by 2022, real GDP had grown by 2.3%, supported by stronger exports of agricultural commodities and a gradual recovery in consumer confidence. Despite these improvements, inflation reached an annual average of 8.2% in 2022, prompting the central bank to raise the benchmark interest rate to 11% in an effort to stabilize prices.

Unemployment has remained a challenge. In mid-2021, the national jobless rate peaked above 14%. The government responded by expanding social assistance programs and introducing job-training initiatives for the growing youth population. In addition, it implemented a “fiscal responsibility law,” which sets constraints on budget deficits while allowing for targeted spending on infrastructure and social welfare. The rationale is to boost long-term growth without triggering unsustainable debt.

Brazil’s export profile is dominated by agricultural products and mineral commodities, including soybeans, coffee, iron ore, and crude oil. Over 40% of export revenue comes from China, followed by markets in the European Union and the United States. However, the reliance on commodities makes Brazil’s external trade vulnerable to fluctuations in global prices. The government aims to diversify by promoting higher-value manufactured exports and expanding service-sector trade, partly through new trade agreements under Mercosur.

Income inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, has fallen slightly over the past decade but remains high by international standards. Government transfer programs and progressive taxation have played a role in reducing poverty, yet rural areas—especially in the north and northeast—continue to lag behind in access to education, healthcare, and formal employment opportunities. Moreover, the introduction of a “price floor” for select agricultural products (such as coffee and sugarcane) is intended to stabilize farmers’ incomes, but critics argue it may distort market signals and lead to inefficiencies.

Environmental concerns have gained urgency in recent years, particularly regarding deforestation in the Amazon region. Brazil’s “Green Growth Plan,” announced in 2022, sets ambitious targets to reduce emissions and protect biodiversity. The plan includes incentives for sustainable agriculture, reforestation projects, and stricter monitoring of illegal logging. However, balancing environmental protection with economic development poses ongoing political and economic challenges.

On the monetary front, the Central Bank of Brazil operates a managed float of the Brazilian real. While the exchange rate often reflects shifts in commodity prices, authorities occasionally intervene using foreign reserve assets to prevent excessive volatility. Foreign direct investment (FDI)—especially in agribusiness, infrastructure, and energy—has grown with support from the “plan”, although critics note that the benefits have not always trickled down to local communities.

To spur growth in the industrial sector, policymakers have launched new supply-side measures, emphasizing research and development (R&D) grants, technology partnerships, and incentives for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). Proponents argue that these policies can diversify the economy beyond raw commodities, while skeptics worry about the fiscal costs and the slow pace of structural reforms.

Overall, Brazil stands at a crossroads. The government continues to grapple with persistent inflation, high unemployment, and unequal development. Yet, the country’s vast natural resources, large domestic market, and ongoing policy reforms offer potential for sustainable and inclusive growth. Whether the new Green Growth Plan and efforts to strengthen fiscal responsibility will translate into long-term stability remains a central question for Brazil’s future trajectory.

**Table 1: Brazil’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2020–2023)**

| Indicator                            | 2020     | 2021     | 2022     | 2023 (est.) |
|--------------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)            | 1420     | 1490     | 1580     | 1640        |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)             | -4.1     | 0.7      | 2.3      | 2.5         |
| Inflation Rate (%)                   | 4.5      | 7.3      | 8.2      | 6.1         |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                | 13.7     | 14.1     | 12.9     | 11.8        |
| Exchange Rate (BRL per US$, average) | 5.15     | 5.35     | 5.10     | 4.95        |

**Table 2: Development and Environmental Indicators**

| Indicator                                                      | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   | 2023 (est.) |
|---------------------------------------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|-------------|
| Gini Coefficient                                              | 0.53   | 0.52   | 0.51   | 0.50        |
| Deforestation Rate (Amazon region, thousand km² cleared/year) | 10.1   | 10.9   | 11.2   | 10.3        |
| Public Spending on “Green Growth Plan” (% of GDP)             | –      | 0.5    | 1.0    | 1.2         |
| Rural Poverty Rate (%)                                        | 23.0   | 22.5   | 21.0   | 20.0        |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                                     | 34.5   | 39.2   | 43.8   | 46.0        |

parts.markscheme: Refers to development or growth that meets present needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs, often integrating environmental, social, and economic considerations
