![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a28e5f9c-1f7b-4c8c-a1d6-260f62976366)
• Additional R&D spending can improve technology and productivity, shifting LRAS to the right over time.  
• This shift reflects an increase in the economy’s productive capacity and potential output.  
• On the AD/AS diagram, the new LRAS curve is drawn to the right of the original LRAS curve, illustrating a higher full employment level of output.  
• In the longer term, the economy can achieve a higher real GDP level without upward pressure on the price level caused by capacity constraints.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, calculate the ratio of income share held by the top 10% compared to the bottom 10%. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Income share of top 10%: 23.5%
- Income share of bottom 10%: 2.9%
- Ratio = 23.5 ÷ 2.9 = 8.1
- Ratio of income share: 8.1

parts.content: Using Table 3 and the information in the text, explain how Finland’s income inequality level could impact its long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• A Gini coefficient of 0.27 indicates relatively low inequality, which can lead to greater social cohesion and higher labor productivity.  
• Social welfare programs and progressive taxes may ensure more equitable access to education and health, raising human capital.  
• These factors can strengthen long-term growth by enhancing the skills base, consumption levels, and overall stability.  
• Excessive equality measures might reduce incentives for entrepreneurship, but Finland’s approach tries to balance equity with economic incentives, supporting sustained growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Finnish government to strengthen economic growth while maintaining low levels of income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Define “economic growth” as a sustained increase in real output over time and define “income inequality” as the unequal distribution of income within a population.  
• Emphasize that Finland’s current low Gini of 0.27 shows relatively high equality, supported by progressive taxation and welfare systems.  
• Propose targeted public investment in human capital, such as increased spending on advanced technical education and vocational training, drawing on Finland’s existing emphasis on research and development.  
• Argue that enhancing specialized education aligns with Finland’s technology-oriented and export-focused industries, boosting labor productivity and innovation in forest products, technology, and machinery.  
• Highlight that such spending can shift the long-run aggregate supply to the right, indicating higher productive capacity; Table 3 suggests a positive multiplier effect, increasing real GDP by more than the initial injection.  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ddf05e40-a20d-46c5-9725-736f7711f014)
• Note that a progressive tax system can fund this investment while keeping income distribution relatively balanced, as higher-income earners contribute a larger share to government revenue.  
• Point out that this maintains equity because it reduces the burden on lower-income groups, preventing a rise in inequality and preserving consumer demand.  
• Acknowledge the risk that higher taxes might disincentivize some investors or highly skilled workers, but Finland’s moderate corporate tax rate of 20% could mitigate this concern.  
• Indicate that linking part of the additional tax revenue to social programs and R&D helps sustain low inequality and encourages research-based growth, consistent with the policy objective mentioned in 2021.  
• Conclude that this approach likely fosters long-term growth and innovation without undermining Finland’s strong track record of income equality.

questions.specification:
Botswana is a landlocked country in Southern Africa. Its economy has historically relied on diamond mining for export revenue, contributing significantly to GDP and government revenue. However, there is growing concern over high unemployment, income inequality, and the need to diversify into other sectors such as tourism and financial services. A recent government infrastructure project, valued at US$200 million, aims to create jobs and stimulate growth. Botswana’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) has been estimated at 0.8. Table 1 and Table 2 provide selected data on Botswana’s economy.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Botswana

| Year | Real GDP (US$ billions) | Unemployment rate (%) | Gini coefficient |  
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2019 | 17.2 | 19.5 | 0.53 |
| 2020 | 15.6 | 22.2 | 0.57 |
| 2021 | 18.0 | 20.0 | 0.55 |

Table 2: Price and Quantity Demanded of Diamond Exports

| Price per carat (Pula) | Quantity demanded (million carats) |
| --- | --- |
| 1000 | 10 |
| 1100 | 9  |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the rate of real GDP growth from 2019 to 2020.
parts.markscheme:
• (15.6 − 17.2) ÷ 17.2 × 100 = −9.3%  
• The rate of real GDP growth is −9.3%.

parts.content: Assuming the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Botswana is 0.8, calculate the total change in real GDP resulting from the government’s US$200 million infrastructure project. Show working.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.8) = 5  
• Total change in real GDP = 5 × 200 million = US$1 000 million

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Botswana’s exported diamonds when the price increases from 1000 Pula per carat to 1100 Pula per carat.
Using the information in Table 2, calculate the change in total revenue from diamond exports as the price increases from 1000 Pula per carat to 1100 Pula per carat.
parts.markscheme:
• Original total revenue = 1000 × 10 = 10 000 million Pula  
• New total revenue = 1100 × 9 = 9 900 million Pula  
• Change in total revenue = 9 900 − 10 000 = −100 million Pula

parts.content: Define the term marginal propensity to consume (MPC).
parts.markscheme: • The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is the fraction of additional income that is spent on consumption.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how a sharp fall in diamond export revenue might affect Botswana’s short-run macroeconomic equilibrium.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/09612283-570a-48a6-986b-fd0f442d6b15)

• A sharp fall in export revenue reduces net exports, leading to a decrease in aggregate demand.  
• In an AD/AS diagram, this is shown by a leftward shift of the AD curve.  
• Real output (GDP) and the price level both tend to fall in the short run compared to the original equilibrium.  
• The economy may experience lower growth and higher unemployment if the lower export revenue persists. 

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the average unemployment rate in Botswana over the period 2019-2021. Show your working.

- Average unemployment rate = 61.7 ÷ 3 = 20.57%
- Average unemployment rate: 20.6% (or 20.57%)

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, explain how high income inequality may act as a barrier to economic development in Botswana.
parts.markscheme:
• Botswana’s Gini coefficient remains high (0.53 in 2019, rising in 2020, then 0.55 in 2021), indicating significant income disparities.  
• High inequality decreases access to education, healthcare, and financial services for lower-income groups.  
• This can prevent many individuals from improving skills or investing in businesses, limiting overall development potential.  
• Large segments of the population may remain in poverty, reducing domestic demand for goods and services and slowing long-term growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the government of Botswana could implement to reduce dependence on diamond export revenue.
parts.markscheme:
• Diversification reduces reliance on a single product and can help stabilize income and employment; a policy focusing on developing tourism and financial services may achieve this.  
• Defining “diversification” indicates shifting resources across multiple industries rather than depending on diamond mining alone.  
• Highlighting “economic development” relates to improving the welfare of citizens through increased incomes, quality of life, and lowering unemployment.  
• Implementing supply-side policies, such as investment in education and training for tourism and financial services, increases the country’s human capital and attracts investment.  
• Improvements to infrastructure support new industries and encourage private sector growth by reducing costs of production, especially for tourism (building roads and airports) and financial services (technological infrastructure).  
• Diagrammatic representation could show a long-run aggregate supply shift rightward as productivity rises, helping to sustain growth beyond diamond mining. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/813dd8a4-ccb6-40c5-8e5a-a26271ae447a)
• Adopting tax incentives for companies entering high-potential sectors encourages entrepreneurship and foreign direct investment outside mining, generating more diverse export revenues.  
• Incorporating information from the text on high unemployment and income inequality identifies that focusing on labor-intensive sectors (e.g., eco-tourism) can create broad-based employment.  
• Ensuring that new industries provide opportunities for both skilled and unskilled workers facilitates more inclusive income distribution and counters high Gini coefficients.  
• Weighing opportunity costs is important, as government resources directed toward new industries imply less funding for other projects, but long-term gains in growth and employment can offset initial expenses.

questions.specification:
## Economic Challenges and Opportunities in Vanuatu

(1) Vanuatu, an archipelago nation in the South Pacific with a population of roughly 320,000, has experienced a mix of economic successes and setbacks over the last few years. Agriculture and tourism are its primary economic drivers, with the export of kava, cocoa, and fish generating important foreign exchange earnings. Tourism, focused on eco-friendly resorts and cultural attractions, has grown steadily in recent years, despite occasional disruptions caused by cyclones and global economic shocks.

(2) Real GDP growth was estimated at 2.5% in 2019 but contracted briefly in 2020 due to border closures, subsequently rebounding to 3.1% in 2021 and 3.8% in 2022. Inflation has remained moderate, rising from 1.0% in 2019 to 2.8% by 2022, although some policy makers remain concerned about increasing fuel and imported food prices. Cyclone Rani, which struck in early 2021, underscored Vanuatu’s vulnerability to climate-related events and highlighted the need for more resilient infrastructure.

(3) In response to rising living costs, the government introduced a new minimum wage in 2021, increasing it by 20%. This policy aimed to protect lower-income workers employed mainly in tourism, retail, and agriculture. Critics argue that the higher minimum wage may increase unemployment if small businesses cannot absorb higher labor costs, whereas supporters believe it may boost domestic consumption and labor productivity.

(4) Foreign direct investment (FDI) in tourism-related ventures has grown, and overseas companies have funded new beachfront resorts and hotels. The government is keen to ensure that the influx of foreign capital provides benefits for local communities, partly by imposing regulations on land development to limit negative externalities such as erosion of coastal habitats.

(5) Public debt has gradually climbed over the past decade, partly due to borrowing to finance large-scale infrastructure projects (such as port expansions and improved inter-island ferry networks). These projects are intended to facilitate trade, attract more visitors, and improve distribution channels for agricultural exports. However, concerns persist about Vanuatu’s ability to service its debt if global conditions deteriorate, especially given the government’s existing commitments to social welfare and disaster response funds.

(6) While the unemployment rate was officially at 6.2% in 2019, it reached 7.5% during the pandemic disruptions and remained around 6.9% in 2022. Seasonal unemployment is common, given the cyclical nature of tourism, which peaks from June to September. Government efforts to diversify the economy include encouraging small-scale manufacturing and expanding digital infrastructure, although progress has been uneven across the islands.

(7) Income inequality, reflected in a significant urban-rural divide, remains a challenge. Some rural communities have limited access to healthcare, education, and financial services. Efforts to reduce inequality have focused on improving transport links between outer islands and the main centers, alongside targeted subsidies for basic necessities such as electricity and water. Still, environmental groups worry that tourism expansion could harm marine ecosystems and threaten the sustainability of both fishing and local livelihoods.

(8) International trade plays a major role in Vanuatu’s economy. Exports of kava and fish are crucial, yet they are subject to volatile world prices. The country also faces the high costs of importing capital goods, processed foods, and fuel. With global travel demand recovering, tourism in Vanuatu is poised for further expansion, but managing this growth so that it fosters equitable development and safeguards the fragile environment will remain a central policy challenge.

Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators for Vanuatu (2019–2022)

| Indicator                     | 2019     | 2020     | 2021     | 2022     |
|------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)     | 2.5      | -1.8     | 3.1      | 3.8      |
| Inflation Rate (%)           | 1.0      | 1.6      | 2.3      | 2.8      |
| Unemployment Rate (%)        | 6.2      | 7.2      | 7.5      | 6.9      |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)       | 45.0     | 48.5     | 50.2     | 51.3     |
| Minimum Wage (VUV/hour)      | 250      | 250      | 300      | 300      |

Table 2: Tourism and Development Indicators for Vanuatu (2019–2022)

| Indicator                           | 2019      | 2020      | 2021      | 2022      |
|-------------------------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| International Tourists (thousands)  | 325       | 100       | 190       | 280       |
| Average Tourist Spending (US$/day)  | 110       | 85        | 95        | 105       |
| Gini Coefficient                    | 0.45      | 0.44      | 0.43      | 0.42      |
| Rural Population below Poverty Line | 24%       | 24%       | 23%       | 22%       |
| FDI Inflows (US$ million)           | 40        | 25        | 30        | 37        |

parts.content:
(a)  
   (i) Define the term public debt as used in the text (paragraph 5).  
   [2 marks]

   (ii) Define the term minimum wage as used in the text (paragraph 3).  
   [2 marks]

(b)  
   (i) Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage point change in the unemployment rate between 2019 and 2022.  
   [2 marks]

   (ii) Sketch an AD/AS diagram to illustrate how changes in aggregate spending might influence Vanuatu’s inflation rate, in reference to the inflation data in Table 1.  
   [3 marks]

(c)  
   Using a business cycle diagram, explain how Vanuatu’s cyclical economic fluctuations (paragraph 6) might affect unemployment.  
   [4 marks]

(d)  
   Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how an increase in international tourist arrivals (Table 2) could affect the exchange rate of the Vanuatu vatu (VUV).  
   [4 marks]

(e)  
   Using a negative externalities diagram, explain how rapid tourism development (paragraph 7) might give rise to environmental costs in Vanuatu.  
   [4 marks]

(f)  
   Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain the change in income inequality indicated by the Gini coefficient data in Table 2.  
   [4 marks]

(g)  
   Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Vanuatu’s expanding tourism sector on the country’s economic growth and development.  
   [15 marks]
parts.markscheme:
(a)  
(i) Public debt is the total amount of money owed by a government to domestic and foreign creditors, typically arising from government borrowing to finance budget deficits or large-scale projects.  

(ii) A minimum wage is a legally established floor on the hourly wage rate that employers must pay workers, designed to protect employees from excessively low pay.

(b)  
(i) The unemployment rate in 2019 is 6.2%, and in 2022 it is 6.9%.  
• Step-by-step calculation:  
  – 6.9% − 6.2% = 0.7 percentage points  
• Therefore, the unemployment rate rose by 0.7 percentage points between 2019 and 2022.

(ii) An AD/AS diagram would normally show an upward-sloping aggregate supply (AS) curve and a downward-sloping aggregate demand (AD) curve.  
• Higher aggregate spending (AD shifting to the right) tends to drive inflation upward, which is consistent with inflation rising from 1.0% in 2019 to 2.8% in 2022.  
• As AD shifts right, the price level increases, illustrating demand-pull inflation.  
• If supply-constraints also exist (for example following cyclone impacts), AS might shift left, creating further upward pressure on the price level.

(c)  
Vanuatu’s cyclical economic fluctuations can lead to periods of expansion and contraction, affecting unemployment.  
• During an economic expansion phase of the business cycle, aggregate demand rises, leading firms to hire more workers, lowering unemployment.  
• During a contraction (such as the pandemic disruption), aggregate demand decreases, firms reduce output, and unemployment rises.  
• In paragraph 6, the seasonal nature of tourism amplifies these cyclical patterns, with unemployment tending to rise during off-peak seasons.  
• The business cycle diagram would show expansions and recessions; peaks in the cycle correspond to lower unemployment, while troughs correspond to higher unemployment.

(d)  
An increase in international tourist arrivals raises demand for the Vanuatu vatu as more tourists exchange foreign currency into VUV.  
• A demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram would have the quantity of VUV on the horizontal axis and the exchange rate (in foreign currency per VUV) on the vertical axis.  
• The shift of the demand curve to the right (due to higher tourist inflows needing VUV) can lead to an appreciation of the VUV.  
• This appreciation may make imports cheaper but could reduce export competitiveness if the currency appreciates substantially.

(e)  
Rapid tourism development can lead to negative externalities, such as damage to marine ecosystems, erosion, or pollution, which impose costs on third parties not reflected in market transactions.  
• A negative externality diagram would show a divergence between marginal private cost (MPC) and marginal social cost (MSC), with MSC lying above MPC due to these external costs.  
• The market equilibrium output of tourism development is higher than the socially optimal level, leading to over-consumption or over-production of tourism facilities.  
• Government regulation (paragraph 4) or environmental protections could reduce the external cost gap between private and social costs.

(f)  
The Lorenz curve shows the distribution of income, and the Gini coefficient is a measure of income inequality, where higher values imply greater inequality.  
• The data in Table 2 shows the Gini coefficient falling from 0.45 in 2019 to 0.42 in 2022, suggesting income inequality has decreased slightly.  
• A Lorenz curve closer to the line of perfect equality indicates a more equal distribution; the downward shift in the Gini coefficient suggests the Lorenz curve may have moved closer to the diagonal.  
• This reduction in inequality could result from improvements in rural incomes, or targeted subsidies and infrastructure projects that narrowed the urban-rural divide.

(g) Using information from the text/data and knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Vanuatu’s expanding tourism sector on the country’s economic growth and development.  
• Definition of economic growth: An increase in real GDP over time.  
• Definition of economic development: A broad-based improvement in key indicators of welfare, such as income distribution, education, healthcare, and environmental sustainability.  
• Definition of tourism sector expansion: Growth in industry activities such as resorts, hotels, and related services that attract foreign visitors.

Points for Discussion and Evaluation (15 marks):

• Potential for Higher Real GDP and Employment  
  – Expanding tourism contributes to aggregate demand, increasing real GDP growth rates (paragraph 2) from -1.8% in 2020 to 3.8% in 2022.  
  – Rising demand for hospitality services may reduce unemployment if businesses hire more workers, although the off-peak season still leads to cyclical and seasonal unemployment.  
  – However, the sector’s vulnerability to shocks (cyclones, pandemics) may limit consistency of employment gains.

• Foreign Exchange Earnings and FDI Inflows  
  – Tourism inflows strengthen Vanuatu’s foreign exchange reserves, potentially stabilizing the currency and facilitating imports of capital goods and fuel (paragraph 8).  
  – FDI in resorts strengthens capital formation (paragraph 4) and may improve infrastructure such as roads and airports, but if foreign investors repatriate profits, the full benefits to Vanuatu may be reduced.

• Improvement in Infrastructure and Local Services  
  – Government borrowing for infrastructure (paragraph 5) supports tourism, potentially raising long-term development by connecting rural agricultural producers with tourist markets.  
  – Better transport links can also improve access to healthcare and education, enhancing overall development.  
  – Over-reliance on debt financing (public debt rising to 51.3% of GDP in 2022) poses risks if global conditions deteriorate.

• Income Distribution and Poverty Reduction  
  – Tourism sector expansion may reduce poverty by creating employment, but inequalities could persist between urban and rural areas (paragraph 7).  
  – The Gini coefficient fell from 0.45 to 0.42, suggesting a slight improvement in income distribution (Table 2), potentially supported by minimum wage policies.  
  – However, some rural communities may benefit less if infrastructural improvements do not reach outer islands.

• Environmental Sustainability Concerns  
  – Rapid tourism development risks ecological damage (paragraph 7), especially in sensitive coastal areas. This can endanger future tourism potential if natural assets are compromised.  
  – Long-term growth may rely on balancing economic gains with environmental protections, aligning with sustainable tourism models.

• Balancing Economic Growth and Development  
  – Tourism can generate high growth in the medium term, raising living standards.  
  – Sustainable development policies, including regulations on land use and environmental protections, are vital to ensure that economic gains do not erode natural resources.  
  – Without careful oversight, tourism expansion can exacerbate regional inequalities if benefits concentrate in specific areas.

Conclusion:  
• Vanuatu’s tourism sector expansion has considerable potential to spur economic growth, boost employment, and attract FDI.  
• The success of tourism-driven development depends on addressing potential pitfalls such as environmental degradation, uneven wealth distribution, and vulnerability to external shocks.  
• With prudent policies—ranging from infrastructure investment to sustainable tourism practices—the sector can serve as a cornerstone for broader development and a more inclusive improvement in living standards.

Still, environmental groups worry that tourism expansion could harm marine ecosystems and threaten the sustainability of both fishing and local livelihoods.

(8) International trade plays a major role in Vanuatu’s economy. Exports of kava and fish are crucial, yet they are subject to volatile world prices. The country also faces the high costs of importing capital goods, processed foods, and fuel. With global travel demand recovering, tourism in Vanuatu is poised for further expansion, but managing this growth so that it fosters equitable development and safeguards the fragile environment will remain a central policy challenge.

parts.content: Define the term public debt as used in the text (paragraph 5).
parts.markscheme: Public debt is the total amount of money owed by a government to domestic and foreign creditors, typically arising from government borrowing to finance budget deficits or large-scale projects.

parts.content: Define the term minimum wage as used in the text (paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme: A minimum wage is a legally established floor on the hourly wage rate that employers must pay workers, designed to protect employees from excessively low pay.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage point change in the unemployment rate between 2019 and 2022.

The unemployment rate in 2019 is 6.2%, and in 2022 it is 6.9%.  
• Step-by-step calculation:  
  – 6.9% − 6.2% = 0.7 percentage points  
• Therefore, the unemployment rate rose by 0.7 percentage points between 2019 and 2022.

parts.content: Using a business cycle diagram, explain how Vanuatu’s cyclical economic fluctuations (paragraph 6) might affect unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b812443d-2df1-40dc-9abe-047defbd42a2)

Vanuatu’s cyclical economic fluctuations can lead to periods of expansion and contraction, affecting unemployment.  
- During an economic expansion phase of the business cycle, aggregate demand rises, leading firms to hire more workers, lowering unemployment.  
- During a contraction (such as the pandemic disruption), aggregate demand decreases, firms reduce output, and unemployment rises.  
- In paragraph 6, the seasonal nature of tourism amplifies these cyclical patterns, with unemployment tending to rise during off-peak seasons.  
- The business cycle diagram would show expansions and recessions; peaks in the cycle correspond to lower unemployment, while troughs correspond to higher unemployment.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how an increase in international tourist arrivals (Table 2) could affect the exchange rate of the Vanuatu vatu (VUV).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/498ad734-909f-4282-85d4-fe6976b41d05)

- An increase in international tourist arrivals raises demand for the Vanuatu vatu (D1 -> D2) as more tourists exchange foreign currency into VUV.  
- A demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram would have the quantity of VUV on the horizontal axis and the exchange rate (in foreign currency per VUV) on the vertical axis.  
- The shift of the demand curve to the right (due to higher tourist inflows needing VUV) can lead to an appreciation of the VUV(e1 to e2)
- This appreciation may make imports cheaper but could reduce export competitiveness if the currency appreciates substantially.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain how rapid tourism development (paragraph 7) might give rise to environmental costs in Vanuatu.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cea44ae6-0f0c-4e7c-8b08-62304538f71f)

- Rapid tourism development can lead to negative externalities, such as damage to marine ecosystems, erosion, or pollution, which impose costs on third parties not reflected in market transactions.  
- A negative externality diagram would show a divergence between marginal private cost (MPC) and marginal social cost (MSC), with MSC lying above MPC due to these external costs.  
- The market equilibrium output (Qm) of tourism development is higher than the socially optimal level (Qopt), leading to over-consumption or over-production of tourism facilities.  
- Government regulation (paragraph 4) or environmental protections could reduce the external cost gap between private and social costs.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a Lorenz curve diagram, explain the change in income inequality indicated by the Gini coefficient data in Table 2.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/99551131-3305-4895-88e2-02dafec94ea2)

- The Lorenz curve shows the distribution of income, and the Gini coefficient is a measure of income inequality, where higher values imply greater inequality.  
- The data in Table 2 shows the Gini coefficient falling from 0.45 in 2019 to 0.42 in 2022, suggesting income inequality has decreased slightly.  
- A Lorenz curve closer to the line of perfect equality indicates a more equal distribution; the downward shift in the Gini coefficient suggests the Lorenz curve may have moved closer to the diagonal.  
- This reduction in inequality could result from improvements in rural incomes, or targeted subsidies and infrastructure projects that narrowed the urban-rural divide.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of Vanuatu’s expanding tourism sector on the country’s economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definitions**
- economic growth: An increase in real GDP over time.  
- economic development: A broad-based improvement in key indicators of welfare, such as income distribution, education, healthcare, and environmental sustainability.  
-  tourism sector expansion: Growth in industry activities such as resorts, hotels, and related services that attract foreign visitors.

**Points for Discussion and Evaluation**:

- Potential for Higher Real GDP and Employment  
  - Expanding tourism contributes to aggregate demand, increasing real GDP growth rates (paragraph 2) from -1.8% in 2020 to 3.8% in 2022.  
  - Rising demand for hospitality services may reduce unemployment if businesses hire more workers, although the off-peak season still leads to cyclical and seasonal unemployment.  
  - However, the sector’s vulnerability to shocks (cyclones, pandemics) may limit consistency of employment gains.

- Foreign Exchange Earnings and FDI Inflows  
  - Tourism inflows strengthen Vanuatu’s foreign exchange reserves, potentially stabilizing the currency and facilitating imports of capital goods and fuel (paragraph 8).  
  - FDI in resorts strengthens capital formation (paragraph 4) and may improve infrastructure such as roads and airports, but if foreign investors repatriate profits, the full benefits to Vanuatu may be reduced.

- Improvement in Infrastructure and Local Services  
  - Government borrowing for infrastructure (paragraph 5) supports tourism, potentially raising long-term development by connecting rural agricultural producers with tourist markets.  
  - Better transport links can also improve access to healthcare and education, enhancing overall development.  
  - Over-reliance on debt financing (public debt rising to 51.3% of GDP in 2022) poses risks if global conditions deteriorate.
  - Infrastructure contributes to new industries and an increase in economic capacity/potential output level, ultimately causing a rightward shift of LRAS1 to LRAS2. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/650948ec-723b-426f-acc0-d5f1cce84a10)

- Income Distribution and Poverty Reduction  
  - Tourism sector expansion may reduce poverty by creating employment, but inequalities could persist between urban and rural areas (paragraph 7).  
  - The Gini coefficient fell from 0.45 to 0.42, suggesting a slight improvement in income distribution (Table 2), potentially supported by minimum wage policies.  
  - However, some rural communities may benefit less if infrastructural improvements do not reach outer islands.

- Environmental Sustainability Concerns  
  - Rapid tourism development risks ecological damage (paragraph 7), especially in sensitive coastal areas. This can endanger future tourism potential if natural assets are compromised.  
  - Long-term growth may rely on balancing economic gains with environmental protections, aligning with sustainable tourism models.

- Balancing Economic Growth and Development  
  - Tourism can generate high growth in the medium term, raising living standards.  
  - Sustainable development policies, including regulations on land use and environmental protections, are vital to ensure that economic gains do not erode natural resources.  
  - Without careful oversight, tourism expansion can exacerbate regional inequalities if benefits concentrate in specific areas.

**Conclusion:**  
- Vanuatu’s tourism sector expansion has considerable potential to spur economic growth, boost employment, and attract FDI.  
- The success of tourism-driven development depends on addressing potential pitfalls such as environmental degradation, uneven wealth distribution, and vulnerability to external shocks.  
- With prudent policies—ranging from infrastructure investment to sustainable tourism practices—the sector can serve as a cornerstone for broader development and a more inclusive improvement in living standards.

questions.specification:
The Philippines is a lower-middle-income country in Southeast Asia comprised of over 7,600 islands. With a population of approximately 110 million in 2021, the country has historically relied on agriculture, services (particularly tourism and business-process outsourcing), and overseas remittances from millions of Filipinos working abroad. Despite steady economic growth prior to 2020, the Philippines continues to face challenges such as high income inequality and reliance on external revenue sources.

The government has launched various infrastructure projects, aiming to improve roads, ports, and public transport networks—measures intended to stimulate growth and reduce regional disparities. In addition, policymakers hope that better infrastructure will encourage higher foreign direct investment (FDI) and boost employment opportunities.

Remittances remain a significant source of income. Official data indicate that money sent home from migrant workers accounts for around 10% of the country’s GDP. Some economists argue that this reliance on external income might slow the development of more robust domestic industries, though others point to the benefits of improved household spending and higher savings due to remittances.

Table 1 shows selected labor market data for 2021, whereas Table 2 provides nominal GDP and price index data for 2019 and 2021. Table 3 outlines the main tax rates in the Philippines. Table 4 provides information on banana exports, one of the Philippines’ leading agricultural exports.

Table 1: Key labor market data for the Philippines (2021)

| Population (millions) | Employed (millions) | Unemployed (millions) |
|-----------------------:|---------------------:|-----------------------:|
|                   110 |               44.41 |                  3.03 |

Table 2: Nominal GDP and price index (2019 = base year 100)

| Year | Nominal GDP (trillion pesos) | Price index (2018=100) |
|-----:|-----------------------------:|------------------------:|
| 2019 |                        16.8 |                    106  |
| 2021 |                        18.5 |                    110  |

Table 3: Tax rates in the Philippines

| Type of tax            | Rate                       |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Corporate income tax   | 25%                        |
| Personal income tax    | Progressive up to 32%      |
| Value-added tax (VAT)  | 12% (on most goods/services)|

Table 4: Banana exports from the Philippines

| Year | Export quantity (million tonnes) | Price (pesos per kg) | Total export revenue (billion pesos) |
|-----:|----------------------------------:|----------------------:|--------------------------------------:|
| 2021 |                             3.60  |                   25  |                                 90.0  |
| 2022 |                             3.40  |                   28  |                                 95.2  |

The Gini coefficient for the Philippines is estimated at about 0.44, suggesting moderate to high levels of income inequality. Meanwhile, government spending on infrastructure—a key policy priority—totaled approximately 300 billion pesos in 2021, intended to stimulate aggregate demand and address the uneven development across the country.

parts.content: Using the data in Table 1, calculate the rate of unemployment in the Philippines for 2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Labor force = 44.41 million + 3.03 million = 47.44 million  
- Unemployment rate = (3.03 ÷ 47.44) × 100 ≈ 6.39%

parts.content: Using the data in Table 4, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Philippine bananas between 2021 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity = ((3.40 – 3.60) ÷ 3.60) × 100 = –5.56%  
- Percentage change in price = ((28 – 25) ÷ 25) × 100 = 12%  
- PED = (–5.56%) ÷ (12%) ≈ –0.46

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the approximate real GDP growth rate for the Philippines from 2019 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Real GDP 2019 = 16.8 ÷ (106 ÷ 100) ≈ 15.85 trillion pesos  
- Real GDP 2021 = 18.5 ÷ (110 ÷ 100) ≈ 16.82 trillion pesos  
- Real GDP growth rate = ((16.82 – 15.85) ÷ 15.85) × 100 ≈ 6.12%

parts.content: A local firm in the Philippines purchases office equipment at a net (before VAT) cost of 100,000 pesos. Using the tax data in Table 3, calculate how much VAT the firm must pay on this purchase.
Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: A progressive tax is one in which the proportion of income paid in tax rises as income increases.

parts.content: Explain why reliance on overseas remittances might limit the country’s long-run economic development.
parts.markscheme:
- Reliance on remittances may reduce domestic investment incentives, as households depend on external income instead of local production.  
- This can lead to slower growth of domestic industries, limiting the development of a more diversified economic base.  
- Migration of skilled workers abroad can create a potential brain drain, diminishing long-run productivity.  
- Over time, spending fueled by remittances might not generate sustainable job creation within the local economy.

parts.content: Using the information provided, calculate the value-added tax (VAT) payable on a purchase worth 300 billion pesos of infrastructure-related goods and services. The standard VAT rate in the Philippines is 12%.
parts.markscheme:
Correct formula:
VAT = 12% of 300 billion pesos
= 0.12 × 300 billion = 36 billion pesos

parts.content: With reference to the information provided, explain two ways in which high income inequality might hinder economic development in the Philippines.
parts.markscheme:
- Limited access to education and health services for lower-income groups constrains their productivity, which slows overall economic progress.  
- Concentrated wealth can reduce broader market demand, as significant parts of the population may lack purchasing power, hindering long-term growth prospects.  
- The Gini coefficient of 0.44 indicates moderate to high inequality, which undermines balanced development and slows poverty reduction.  
- Regions with lower incomes might miss out on infrastructure and investment, perpetuating uneven growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of the Philippines could implement to encourage greater export growth and reduce unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
- Exports refer to domestically produced goods and services sold abroad, and unemployment refers to the situation where a portion of the labor force is without work. A recommendation is to lower corporate taxes for export-oriented firms.  
- Reductions in the corporate tax rate from 25% to a lower rate could incentivize both domestic and foreign firms to expand operations in key sectors such as agricultural exports (e.g., bananas) and manufacturing, benefiting from economies of scale.  
- The policy would increase export competitiveness by allowing firms to retain more profits for reinvestment, potentially leading to more jobs and production aimed at foreign markets.
- This can be shown in the diagram by a rightward shift of the SRAS (and possibly AD), leading to higher output and lower unemployment without significant inflation.
 ![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f4a6545c-b02b-4b26-b124-2c7c26658ad4)
- Higher production for export markets could help reduce unemployment by absorbing more of the 3.03 million unemployed workers, as indicated in Table 1.  
- Greater output could then be sold internationally, aligning with data showing significant potential in agricultural exports (Table 4) and the government’s focus on infrastructure development.  
- Linking the tax incentive to performance criteria (e.g., minimum export targets) would ensure that benefits directly bolster export-led growth and result in sustainable employment gains.  
- With 300 billion pesos designated for infrastructure spending, improved logistics can further reduce production costs and enhance export capacity, reinforcing the positive impact of a tax incentive.  
- Benefits include attracting more foreign direct investment, boosting demand for local resources, and diversifying industries beyond reliance on remittances.  
- A possible challenge involves reduced tax revenue in the short run, limiting public funds for social programs, but higher levels of output and employment could expand the future tax base and offset initial losses.  
- This policy can encourage a more robust export sector, address unemployment, and support the Philippines’ long-run growth objectives by diversifying sources of national income.

questions.specification:
Iceland, with a population of approximately 376 000 in 2022, has a highly developed economy that relies on fisheries, tourism, and aluminum smelting as its main industries. Tourism emerged as the most rapidly expanding sector over the last decade, surpassing fish exports as the largest export earner. However, fish exports remain vital to employment and economic stability.

Despite relatively high standards of living and a strong social welfare system, Iceland’s income inequality has fluctuated. The country’s Gini coefficient has risen and fallen in recent years, partly reflecting changes in taxation policies and variations in household income from tourism. The government imposes progressive personal income taxes, along with corporate and value-added taxes. In 2022, economists in Iceland estimated that the average marginal propensity to consume (MPC) was 0.8.

Table 1: Iceland’s Real GDP and Gini Coefficient

| Year | Real GDP (billion ISK) | Gini Coefficient |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 2020 | 2 490                  | 0.27            |
| 2021 | 2 580                  | 0.29            |
| 2022 | 2 675                  | 0.28            |

Table 2: Price and Quantity Demanded of Fresh Cod in the Domestic Market (2021)

| Price (ISK/kg) | Quantity Demanded (tonnes per month) |
| --- | --- |
| 1 600          | 12 000                               |
| 1 800          | 10 800                               |

Table 3: Selected Tax Rates in Iceland


| Type of tax            | Rate of tax                         |
| ---                    | ---                                 |
| Corporate income tax   | 20 %                                |
| Personal income tax    | Progressive rates up to 46 %        |
| Value-added tax (VAT)  | 24 %, 11 %, 0 % (depending on goods) |

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for fresh cod when the price increases from ISK 1 600 per kg to ISK 1 800 per kg.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = (10 800 − 12 000) ÷ 12 000 × 100 % = −10 %  
• Percentage change in price = (1 800 − 1 600) ÷ 1 600 × 100 % = 12.5 %  
• PED = (−10 %) ÷ 12.5 % = −0.8 (the absolute value is 0.8)

parts.content: Using Table 1, calculate Iceland’s average annual growth rate in real GDP from 2020 to 2022. Show all working.
parts.markscheme:
• Real GDP in 2020 = 2 490; Real GDP in 2022 = 2 675  
• Growth factor over two years = 2 675 ÷ 2 490 ≈ 1.0743  
• Average annual growth rate = (1.0743^(1/2) − 1) × 100 % ≈ 3.64 %

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how growth in net exports from tourism might affect the Icelandic economy in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/653951ad-987a-48a8-9b76-5bc717f8f934)
• Additional export revenue from tourism raises aggregate demand, shifting AD to the right.  
• Higher AD tends to increase real output (real GDP) and creates upward pressure on the general price level.  
• Rising tourism earns foreign currency and boosts real incomes, promoting consumer spending and stimulating economic activity.  
• In the short run, producers respond by increasing output, which can lead to economic growth and a lower unemployment rate.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Assume the Icelandic government imposes a VAT of 11% on fresh cod. Calculate the final price a consumer would pay per kg if the market price before tax is ISK 1 800.
parts.markscheme:
VAT = 11% of ISK 1 800
 = (11/100) × 1 800 = ISK 198

Final price = 1 800 + 198 = ISK 1 998 per kg

parts.content: With reference to the data in Table 1, explain how changes in the Gini coefficient from 2020 to 2022 might reflect shifts in Iceland’s income distribution.
parts.markscheme:
• The Gini coefficient rose from 0.27 to 0.29 between 2020 and 2021, indicating a temporary widening in income inequality.  
• Between 2021 and 2022 it fell to 0.28, suggesting a reduction in income disparity.  
• These movements could be linked to fluctuations in incomes derived from Iceland’s key exports (fisheries and tourism) and changing taxation policies.  
• A falling Gini coefficient often suggests more even distribution of income, whereas an increasing Gini suggests relatively greater inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the government of Iceland could introduce in order to reduce the level of income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality describes how unevenly income is distributed, and the Gini coefficient (ranging from 0.27 to 0.29 and then 0.28) measures the extent of that inequality.  
• A progressive tax ensures that as income rises, the proportion of income paid in taxes increases, which can narrow income gaps when properly structured.  
• Additional government spending on services like education or vocational training can raise the incomes of lower-skilled workers, aligning with Iceland’s high MPC of 0.8, so each krona spent potentially circulates multiple times in the economy to benefit lower-income groups.  
• The combination of progressive taxation (up to 46 % for higher incomes) and social spending can reduce disparities, since those who benefit most from strong tourism or fish exports contribute more to government revenues.  
• By channeling these revenues into targeted assistance—such as scholarships for industry-specific courses or subsidies to lower-income fishery communities—greater equity is achieved, consistent with Iceland’s strong social welfare objectives.  
• The approach could help stabilize the Gini coefficient at lower levels, mitigating the fluctuations shown in Table 1 by compensating households facing income volatility from seasonal changes in tourism.  
• The multiplier effect associated with the 0.8 MPC suggests that fiscal transfers or government outlays amplify total spending, further boosting incomes at the lower end and stimulating domestic demand for goods beyond tourism and fisheries.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e207f85c-4fa7-44e2-9157-c7d6b3012668)
• Iceland’s relatively small population means appropriately scaled measures in education and job support can have a meaningful impact, improving long-term productivity and wage potential.  
• This policy might entail opportunity costs, as funds allocated to training or services could have been used elsewhere, but the social benefits of reducing inequality (more social cohesion, less strain on welfare systems) can justify the expense.  
• A carefully administered policy, using progressive taxes and increased social expenditures, can help Iceland maintain stable growth while ensuring more equitable income distribution.

questions.specification:
Ukraine has historically been a major exporter of agricultural products, especially wheat and corn. In 2021, Ukraine accounted for approximately 10% of global wheat exports. However, economic disruptions in recent years have led to concerns about overreliance on primary goods exports and volatility in export earnings.  
In addition, Ukraine’s industrial output has fluctuated, with steel and metal product exports also contributing significantly to its balance of trade. Government officials are considering new policies to diversify exports and stabilize the economy.  
Macroeconomic indicators have varied between 2019 and 2021. The COVID‐19 pandemic and geopolitical tensions have contributed to changes in real GDP growth, tax revenues, and income distribution. The Ukrainian government has increased spending on infrastructure to support economic recovery. Some economists estimate Ukraine’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) remains relatively high, suggesting the potential for a strong Keynesian multiplier effect.

Table 1: Ukraine’s Selected Macroeconomic Data (2019–2021)  
| Year | Real GDP (UAH billions) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) | Gini Coefficient | Planned Gov’t Infrastructure Spending (UAH billions) |  
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |  
| 2019 | 4200 | 3.5 | 0.26 | 30 |  
| 2020 | 4060 | –3.3 | 0.27 | 40 |  
| 2021 | 4205 | 3.6 | 0.28 | 50 |  

In 2021, government spending on infrastructure reached UAH 50 billion, aiming to rebuild roads, railways, and improve digital connectivity. The government is also seeking to improve tax collection to finance these expenditures.  

Table 2: Ukraine’s Tax Revenues by Type (2021)  
| Tax Category                       | Revenue (UAH billions) |  
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------- |  
| Personal Income Tax (progressive)  | 300                    |  
| Corporate Income Tax               | 200                    |  
| Value Added Tax (VAT)              | 270                    |  
| Import Duties & Others             | 130                    |  
| Total Tax Revenue                  | 900                    |  

Meanwhile, income inequality has narrowed only slightly, with the Gini coefficient drifting upward from 0.26 to 0.28 between 2019 and 2021. While the overall standard of living has shown some improvement, differences between rural and urban incomes remain substantial.  

Ukraine’s wheat market is illustrated in Figure 1 and Table 3 below. Wheat demand is relatively price‐elastic in some importing nations, but fluctuating world prices have created uncertainty for agricultural producers.  

Table 3: Price and Quantity Demanded for Ukrainian Wheat  
| Price per ton (US$) | Quantity Demanded (millions of tons) |  
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------- |  
| 250                 | 2.0                                   |  
| 275                 | 1.8                                   |  

Figure 1 (conceptual only): Demand and Supply for Ukrainian Wheat  
• D indicates Ukraine’s wheat demand (domestic + exports)  
• S indicates Ukraine’s wheat supply  
• World price in 2021 averaged about US\$250 per ton, increasing to US\$275 per ton in 2022

parts.content: Using the information from Table 3, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Ukrainian wheat when the price increases from US\$250 per ton to US$275 per ton.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(1.8 – 2.0) / 2.0] × 100% = –10%  
• Percentage change in price = [(275 – 250) / 250] × 100% = 10%  
• PED = (–10%) / (10%) = –1.0

parts.content: Using the data in Table 1, calculate the value of the Keynesian multiplier if Ukraine’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.75.
Using information from Table 1, calculate the real GDP growth rate between 2020 and 2021 and show your working.
Using information from Table 2, calculate the percentage share of personal income tax (UAH 300 billion) in total tax revenue for Ukraine in 2021.
Define the term “Keynesian multiplier.”
parts.markscheme: • The Keynesian multiplier is the factor by which an initial injection into the economy leads to a greater final increase in national income.

parts.content: Explain why Ukraine’s reliance on primary goods exports (such as wheat) may be considered a source of economic vulnerability for the country.
parts.markscheme:
• Heavy dependence on primary goods exposes the economy to volatile global commodity prices, which can lead to fluctuating export revenues.  
• An adverse change in world market conditions or demand can cause sudden drops in foreign earnings and reduce government revenue.  
• It limits diversification, making it harder to develop manufacturing and service sectors for stable, long‐term growth.  
• Dependence on farming and extractive industries can leave the economy vulnerable to external shocks such as poor harvests or global price downturns.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the percentage of Ukraine's total tax revenue that comes from Value Added Tax (VAT) in 2021. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- VAT revenue ÷ Total tax revenue × 100
- 270 ÷ 900 × 100 = 30.0%
- Percentage of total tax revenue from VAT: 30.0%

parts.content: Using information from Table 1 and the Gini coefficient data, explain how a rise in the Gini coefficient from 0.26 to 0.28 might reflect changes in income inequality in Ukraine.
parts.markscheme:
• The Gini coefficient measures income inequality, and an increase suggests that the distribution of income may have become more unequal.  
• Even though real GDP has recovered from its dip in 2020, the benefit might be unevenly distributed across the population.  
• The infrastructure spending could have contributed to overall growth but not necessarily improved rural and urban income gaps.  
• Persistent disparities between regions could be causing the slight upward trend in the coefficient, indicating growing inequality.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the Ukrainian government could implement to promote sustainable economic recovery and address the challenges of income inequality. In the response, justify the recommendation in the context of Ukraine’s macroeconomic situation.
parts.markscheme:
• Definition: “Income inequality” is the unequal distribution of earnings across the population. “Progressive taxation” is a tax system in which the tax rate increases as taxable income increases.  
• Definition: “Sustainable economic recovery” refers to growth strategies that ensure stable expansion without large negative social or environmental consequences.  
• Explanation/Economic Theory: A policy designed to address income inequality and foster long‐term recovery can be a targeted expansionary fiscal policy with continued investment in infrastructure and progressive tax reforms.  
• This approach increases aggregate demand, reflected by rightward shifts in AD, leveraging Ukraine’s relatively high MPC (Table 1) to generate a stronger multiplier effect.  
• Improved infrastructure enhances productive capacity and can shift LRAS rightward, facilitating long‐term growth and a more diversified economy beyond primary goods exports (case study context). 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9621f376-0a44-4746-8b82-998a5b90ae0f)
• A more progressive tax structure enhances government revenue (Table 2 shows significant potential in personal income tax) and redistributes income, reducing the Gini coefficient over time.  
• Properly channeled funds toward skill development, urban‐rural connectivity, and industrial diversification can support this policy, as indicated by the government’s plan to reduce reliance on primary exports.  
• Evaluation: Higher infrastructure spending can increase deficits in the short run but is justified if it leads to greater employment and stable GDP growth.  
• Targeted social programs, financed by progressive taxes, can narrow the Gini coefficient, addressing the upward trend from 0.26 to 0.28.  
• The policy can create a more inclusive growth pattern: improving incomes in rural areas and reducing vulnerability to commodity price swings.  
• Conclusion: Shifting fiscal resources toward infrastructure, coupled with equitable tax measures, fosters both macroeconomic stability and a fairer distribution of income, promoting sustainable recovery in Ukraine’s context.

questions.specification:
## Balancing Technology-Led Growth and Social Equity in South Korea 

South Korea is widely recognized for its rapid industrialization and highly skilled workforce. From the late 20th century onward, the country experienced fast-paced economic growth under export-led strategies focusing on heavy industries, consumer electronics, automobiles, and semiconductors. In 2021, exports accounted for approximately 37% of the country’s GDP, reflecting the ongoing importance of global trade. However, slowing worldwide demand and rising competition have prompted policymakers to explore new areas such as green technology, biomedical research, and artificial intelligence to sustain long-term growth.

Despite its advanced economy, South Korea has grappled with income inequality and an aging population. According to official statistics, roughly 15% of senior citizens live in relative poverty, one of the highest rates among OECD nations. The government introduced targeted social welfare policies, partly funded through fiscal stimulus, to support low-income households. In 2022, roughly 40 trillion KRW (around US$32 billion) was allocated to health-care, pensions, and housing for the elderly, marking an 8% increase from the previous year. Critics argue that while these measures help in the short run, structural issues such as the high cost of living and stagnant wages in certain sectors remain unresolved.

In addition to social policies, the Bank of Korea has occasionally engaged in managed exchange rate interventions to stabilize the Korean won against major currencies. Between 2019 and 2021, the won experienced moderate volatility due to shifts in global capital flows, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. As part of monetary policy tightening, the central bank raised interest rates in 2021 to address inflationary pressures fueled by global supply chain disruptions and strong domestic demand for real estate. Nonetheless, the Consumer Price Index (CPI) increased by an average of 3.2% that year, prompting ongoing debates over whether further interventions are necessary.

A major focus of South Korea’s current administration involves fostering growth in creative industries and advanced manufacturing. According to government sources, spending on Research and Development (R&D) as a percentage of GDP reached 4.8% in 2021, among the highest in the world. Many of these R&D initiatives concentrate on green technologies, partly in response to the country’s pledge to achieve carbon neutrality by 2050. Local firms in steel, petrochemicals, and automobile manufacturing are under pressure to adopt cleaner production methods. Although government subsidies for renewable energy projects have increased, critics are calling for clearer policies on carbon taxation, as well as regulatory measures that would make polluting firms internalize negative externalities.

Meanwhile, rising private education costs have been at the center of public debate. Household spending on private tutoring exceeded 23 trillion KRW in 2021. Some economists argue that private tutoring represents a demerit good if it incentivizes unsustainable household borrowing and creates pressure on students, while others claim it is a form of human capital investment. To address soaring education expenses, the government introduced a partial subsidy scheme for after-school programs, aiming to reduce inequality in educational access. The official data indicate that participation in these subsidized programs has increased by 12% nationally since their introduction.

South Korea’s ambitious trade policies also drive changes in domestic markets. Free trade agreements with the United States, the European Union, and multiple ASEAN members have lowered tariffs, boosting exports of electronics and automobiles. However, domestic farmers and small-scale producers often express concerns about import competition. The government responded by providing financial support such as low-interest loans for upgrading technology and marketing strategies. Although this has helped certain agricultural cooperatives modernize, critics argue that long-term competitiveness requires structural reforms, not just subsidies.

Policymakers are increasingly aware of potential market failures arising from rapid urbanization. The concentration of industries and jobs in metropolitan Seoul has led to congestion and surging real estate prices. In 2021, apartment prices in Seoul rose by 16% on average, fueling debates on property taxes and rent controls. While city planners have proposed public transport expansions and new satellite cities, economists warn that these projects could lead to oversupply in some areas if not carefully timed. Balancing the immediate need for housing with long-term spatial planning remains a challenge.

Despite these complexities, South Korea continues to show signs of resilience. The current account has remained in surplus since 1998, and foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows are rebounding after a slowdown in 2020. Yet, as global supply chains evolve and new challenges like climate change and digital transformation emerge, the nation’s policymakers face a critical juncture. Implementing cohesive strategies ranging from prudent monetary policies and targeted fiscal spending to effective environmental regulations and social safety nets will likely determine whether South Korea can maintain its status as a global economic leader.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2021)**

| Indicator                              | 2018    | 2019    | 2020    | 2021    |
|----------------------------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)             | 1,619   | 1,647   | 1,630   | 1,766   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)              | 2.7     | 2.0     | -0.9    | 4.1     |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                 | 3.8     | 4.0     | 4.5     | 4.0     |
| CPI Inflation Rate (%)                | 1.5     | 0.4     | 0.5     | 3.2     |
| Budget Balance (% of GDP)             | 1.6     | 0.9     | -2.2    | -1.5    |
| Exchange Rate (KRW per US$)           | 1,100   | 1,160   | 1,200   | 1,155   |

**Table 2: Data on Human Capital and Trade**

| Indicator                              | 2018    | 2019    | 2020    | 2021    |
|----------------------------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| R&D Spending (% of GDP)                | 4.5     | 4.6     | 4.7     | 4.8     |
| Private Tutoring Expenditure (trillion KRW) | 19.0    | 20.2    | 21.1    | 23.0    |
| High-Tech Exports (US$ billion)        | 570     | 582     | 559     | 610     |
| Agricultural Imports (US$ billion)     | 27      | 28      | 32      | 35      |

parts.content: Define the term “demerit good,” referenced in the text (Paragraph 5)
parts.markscheme: Goods that are not socially desirable but are over provided by the market.

parts.content: Define “relative poverty” as used in the text (Paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme: The inability to afford the standard of goods and services considered typical in their society.

The increase in nominal GDP between 2019 and 2021:

- Nominal GDP in 2021 = US$1,766 billion

parts.content: Using an externalities diagram, explain how government subsidies for renewable energy could address negative externalities from traditional industries (Paragraph 4).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2bc679d4-e4cf-4181-b5ed-e4375811d729)

- Negative externalities arise when production or consumption imposes costs on third parties (e.g., pollution from conventional industries). This is shown by the vertical distance between the MSC and S = MPC curves in the diagram.
- A subsidy lowers the cost of producing renewable energy, shifting the supply curve for “clean energy” to the right and reducing reliance on polluting methods. This is indirectly represented by the reduction in quantity from Qm to Qopt, as cleaner energy replaces conventional sources.
- This shift reduces over-reliance on fossil fuels, decreasing the external costs to society and moving the market closer to the socially optimal output level where MSC = MSB. 

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram, explain how an increase in agricultural imports (noted in Table 2) might affect the domestic market for farm products in South Korea.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3817e6d3-5bae-4b17-8aa5-171945508e23)

- As imports rise, there is an increase in overall supply in the domestic market, a shift from S1 to S2. 
- The supply curve for farm products in South Korea shifts to the right (S₁ to S₂) or domestic producers face downward pressure on prices from P₁ to P₂ (see the movement along the demand curve from point A to point B). 
- This leads to a lower equilibrium price and quantity for domestic producers, assuming demand remains unchanged.

- On a Lorenz curve diagram, greater inequality may be shown by the curve bowing further away from the line of perfect equality, causing a shift from L1 to L2. 
- More private tutoring for higher-income households can widen the gap between high- and low-income groups in the long run, as education differentials translate into income differentials.

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how managed exchange rate interventions by the Bank of Korea can influence the value of the won.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/768641ea-89c2-4077-b174-74d0478ac181)

- The central bank buys or sells the won in foreign exchange markets to influence the currency’s value.  
- Buying won (and selling foreign reserves) causes a shift of the supply/demand curves for the won, appreciating its value; selling won (and buying foreign reserves) depreciates it.  
- On an exchange rate diagram (price of won on the vertical axis, quantity on the horizontal), the intervention shifts the relevant curve, moving the exchange rate to a targeted level. 
- Following the pandemic Demand for Won decreases to D2, however, Bank of Korea increased interest rates, to increase demand and return to the original value of Won on D1, with the exchange rate e1.  

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 
<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the impact of South Korea’s government policies and projects on its future economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition:**

- Economic growth: an increase in real output over time, commonly measured by GDP.  
- Economic development: improvements in living standards, reduction in poverty, greater access to education and healthcare, and general welfare enhancements.  
- Policy measures discussed: fiscal stimulus (social welfare, subsidies), monetary policy (interest rates, exchange rate), industrial policies (R&D spending, green technology), and structural reforms.  

**Range of Arguments:**  

1. Technological Advancements and R&D Investments  
- High R&D spending of 4.8% of GDP (Table 2) can foster innovation, expand high-tech exports, and promote long-term growth.  
- May increase productivity in emerging sectors like AI, green tech, and biomedical research, laying the groundwork for new industries.  
- However, there could be an opportunity cost if funds are directed away from other social priorities (e.g., housing, pensions).  
- In the long run, if technological successes are widespread, overall development (improved living standards, job opportunities) could rise significantly.  

2. Social Welfare and Income Distribution  
- Government spending on healthcare, pensions, and housing for the elderly increased by 8% from 2021 to 2022 (Paragraph 2), supporting vulnerable groups in the short run.  
- This may reduce relative poverty (currently 15% of the elderly) and improve social equity, aligning with development goals.  
- Structural issues such as high living costs and stagnant wages in some sectors could hinder inclusive growth if not addressed.  
- Long-term policies aimed at reducing income disparities and ensuring a more equitable distribution of resources are crucial for sustainable development.  

3. Green Technologies and Negative Externalities  
- Subsidies for renewables (Paragraph 4) reduce reliance on polluting industries, potentially improving health outcomes and reducing environmental damage.  
- Achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 can further position the economy for environmentally sustainable growth, appealing to investors concerned with ESG (Environmental, Social, and Governance) factors.  
- Without additional measures like carbon taxation (mentioned by critics), polluters may continue contributing to negative externalities, potentially undermining the sustainability aspect of growth.  
- Transitioning can be costly in the short term, requiring retraining and equipment upgrades, yet it may result in higher-quality growth over time.  
- Subsidies to promote renewable energy production decrease supply of non-renewable energy sources, leading to production at Qopt. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2bc679d4-e4cf-4181-b5ed-e4375811d729)
4. Private Tutoring and Human Capital  
- Private tutoring, at 23 trillion KRW in 2021 (Table 2), may raise educational attainment for those who can afford it and improve long-term productivity for individuals.  
- However, unequal access could exacerbate income inequality, as low-income families may not afford the same level of extra education.  
- Government subsidy schemes for after-school programs (Paragraph 5) might provide some relief, but if private spending remains a dominant force, inequality could worsen.  
- In the long run, if disparities in education widen, the uneven distribution of higher-paying job opportunities might limit overall development.  

5. Trade Policies and Impact on Local Producers  
- FTAs with the US, EU, and ASEAN (Paragraph 6) contribute to export growth (electronics, automobiles) but expose domestic farmers to more competition (agricultural imports rose to US$35 billion by 2021, Table 2).  
- Short-term financial support (loans, subsidies) helps farmers, but sustainability might require deeper structural reforms such as modernization or diversification of agricultural practices.  
- In the long run, maintaining competitiveness can support stable income for rural communities and avoid potential increases in rural poverty, contributing to balanced development.  

6. Monetary Policy and Managed Exchange Rates  
- Interest rate hikes (Paragraph 3) address inflation, which reached 3.2% in 2021, preventing purchasing power erosion.  
- Managed exchange rates stabilize export revenues, particularly important given 37% of GDP comes from exports (Paragraph 1).  
- Overly frequent interventions might deplete foreign reserves or distort market signals.  
- Stability in inflation and exchange rates can foster investor confidence and support steady economic growth, but must be balanced with allowing market mechanisms to function effectively.  

**Conclusion:**

- South Korea’s mix of policies innovative R&D, social welfare expansions, green initiatives, and active trade strategies has strong potential to maintain robust economic growth and enhance development.  
- The main challenge lies in addressing structural inequalities (e.g., elderly poverty, high cost of living, unequal educational opportunities) alongside fostering a greener economy.  
- Effective use of subsidies, prudent exchange rate interventions, and well-targeted social spending can promote long-term sustainability, but require careful planning and monitoring of opportunity costs.  
- If successfully implemented, these policies can consolidate South Korea’s position as an advanced economy with improved social and environmental outcomes, reflecting both quantitative growth and broader developmental goals.

questions.specification:
## Taiwan’s Economy: Stability Amid Shifts

Taiwan, an island economy in East Asia, has long been recognized for its advanced manufacturing sector, particularly in semiconductors and consumer electronics. Over the past decade, Taiwan’s real GDP growth has fluctuated between 2% and 6% annually, influenced by global demand cycles for high-tech exports and evolving trade policies. Meanwhile, the government has prioritized initiatives to stabilize inflation, which typically hovers around 1.5–2.0% but has recently crept above 2% due to higher energy prices.

Reflecting its outward orientation, Taiwan maintains extensive trade relationships with large economies in North America, Europe, and Asia. However, trade tensions particularly concerning electronic components have occasionally led Taiwanese firms to consider shifting production overseas or diversifying export markets. Concurrently, policymakers have employed selective tariffs on specific imports if they suspect dumping practices that could harm local producers. The government also encourages research and development (R&D) through tax incentives, hoping to maintain Taiwan’s competitive edge in emerging technologies.

On the domestic front, Taiwan faces questions about wage stagnation and competition from lower-cost producers in Southeast Asia. While unemployment remains relatively low (hovering around 3.8% most years), underemployment and concerns over job quality persist. In recent years, the government introduced “Productivity Up” measures, offering subsidies for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) to adopt automation or improve efficiency. Proponents of these policies argue that they sustain higher-value-added production in Taiwan, while critics fear that some manufacturing jobs could move abroad if labor costs rise too quickly.

Another challenge involves income inequality. Although Taiwan’s Gini coefficient has declined slightly over the past five years (from 0.34 to 0.33), the wealth gap between urban centers like Taipei and more rural areas remains significant. In response, policy debates revolve around whether to expand progressive taxation or provide targeted social services, such as housing allowances and improved health-care in underdeveloped regions. Advocates claim that these measures bolster social cohesion, while detractors worry about ballooning public expenditure.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) has played a key role in Taiwan’s economic development strategy. Technology giants from the United States, Japan, and Europe invest in joint R&D ventures with Taiwanese firms. Yet some local businesses express concern that larger multinational corporations could overshadow domestic innovations. Policymakers attempt to balance openness to global capital with initiatives that elevate local suppliers, especially in critically important sectors like semiconductor fabrication and green energy.

Taiwan’s monetary authorities maintain a managed float exchange rate system, intervening intermittently to address excessive currency volatility. A stable New Taiwan dollar (NTD) supports export competitiveness but can also attract speculative inflows. The central bank keeps a close eye on foreign exchange reserves, which stood at around US$550 billion in 2022 one of the highest in the world relative to GDP. Looking ahead, experts see Taiwan’s economic fortunes hinging on a combination of steady global demand for electronics, prudent monetary and fiscal policies, and success in raising domestic consumption to offset potential downturns in external demand.

Further:

- Taiwan’s annual FDI inflows have averaged around US 11 billion over the past four years.  
- Latest energy subsidies reached TWD 20 billion (approximately US 665 million), as part of the “Productivity Up” measures.
